issue_comment_body:
> here is a lot of hard coding of Main Zone in rxv code

True, there are a lot of hardcoded strings that have MainZone  as substring due to the fact that:
1) our receiver is single zone one
2) when we developed initial version of this lib, we (@jnnt and I) only wanted to put a nice'ish API on top of calls that we reverse engineered from sniffed traffic. But OTOH it should be pretty straightforward to parametrize them with "{zone}/...". 

>  which is a new top level find call that returns an RXV object for each zone discovered

Having separate objects to interact with do look nice from homeassistant's point of view (since you would not have to touch HA<->rxv integration). But I'm not fully convinced TBH.  A new method  returning a list of Zone objects that are API compatible also seems nice.

Please remove the six dependency.

> We should discuss the technical debt building up. I know we only want to use a subset of the GeoTrans C library but we're doing a lot of workarounds to avoid having to make all the GeoTrans C code into a package.

I'm not sure what you mean by technical debt. This library has been around for 5 years, and it only does one thing. A full wrap of GeoTrans, while it might be nice, would be a lot of work. That library's arrangement isn't very convenient to do it either.

> The approach that I saw in that branch looks good to me. I'd recommend leaving dict as the base class, unless you have something specific driving the choice to make it something else.

Oh, and even if you decide that you have valid case for _not_ inheriting from dict, make sure it at least inherits from object, to ensure we're using a new-style class there in Python 2.

Improved search performance and capability is awesome. Elasticsearch is likewise good tech.  overall, I like where this is going.

Default behavior for the /api/task url is to use the celery backend to return task results.  The api is just a thin wrapper around the celery app -- same user experience basically as just using the python library with async result either Pending or returing the result. I would not switch this over to use Elasticsearch -- I would only use elasticsearch for the dashboard Task results view.   My reasoning is that there are a lot of reasons that the elasticsearch data could be missing or inaccurate -- missed events, ingestion delays, failure scenarios etc.  However, the celery backend should always have the tombstone record, or return Pending.

it would be great to refactor the whole event cache into a provider model.  Support the out-of-box in memory Event state, but also support other models, e.g. elasticsearch, mongodb, even in-mem but over a socket.  I have never liked the event state cache sharing fate with Flower -- it makes restarts painful, hard to keep it consistent across multiple flower instances, etc.

Another thing to think about is looking up a task by ID in the dashboard. If a task is not found in the flower "cache", flower task view can fall back to just getting the tombstone record from the backend.  See my PR https://github.com/mher/flower/pull/772

This is what I use in `shell_config/init.bash`:

```
if [ -n "$VIRTUAL_ENV" ]; then
    PREFIX=$(basename $VIRTUAL_ENV)
    if [ -n "$VENV_PROMPT" ]; then
        PREFIX=$(printf "$VENV_PROMPT" "$PREFIX")
    else
        PREFIX="$PREFIX "
    fi
    PS1="\[\033[01;34m\]\$PREFIX\e[0m$PS1"
fi
```
This adds a space to avoid the jamming of the venv name, and provides an easy way to configure e.g. parens around the name:

I get it, "For Humans" and all of that.   The problem is that humans are going to screw up exception handling.   For one, they're lazy and prone to wrapping things with a diaper exception:

    t = await spawn(something_with_http_and_database_and_crypto_and_cloud)
    try:
        result = await t.join()
    except:
         print("Welp. Shrug. ")

Or they're forgetful and don't check exceptions at all:

    t = await spawn(something_with_http_and_database_and_crypto_and_cloud)
    result = await t.join()

Or they screw up something with layering of exceptions:

    async def spam():
            return await timeout_after(5, something_with_http_and_database_and_crypto_and_cloud)

    t = await spawn(spam)
    try:
        result = await timeout_after(20, spam)
    except TaskTimeout:
         print("Which timeout?!?")

Spawning a task is not like a normal function call.  A spawned task runs concurrently in its own context. The Curio `join()` method doesn't mix contexts when it comes to reporting exceptions.  The spawned task either runs successfully or it doesn't.  If it doesn't, there is a single exception, `TaskError` that gets raised by `join()` in response to that.   If someone wants more information about what happened, they can inspect the exception cause.   If any other kind of exception comes out of `join()`, it's either an exception related to the `join()` operation itself (e.g., cancellation, timeout, etc.) or it's a bug in Curio.   There is no ambiguity about what it is.

If someone doesn't want exceptions wrapped in a `TaskError`, they can always do this as well:

    t = await spawn(some_task)
    await t.wait()
    try:
         r = t.result
    except WhateverError:
         ...
Fixed.

Oh, and if you want to get serious about Windows and add IOCP support (#68), then this has a whole bundle of surprising traps; my current understanding is summarized here: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/52

There are a number of possible approaches here. Most existing event loops
don't provide a "pause" method for this purpose, but instead provide a kind
of "pump" interface, where you can say "run for one iteration then return".
(Often with some flag to control whether the loop should block if nothing
is ready to run,  versus forcing the selector timeout to zero.) These
approaches are still pretty awkward because they rely on polling, though.
The next level of integration is to directly use the GUI event loop for I/o
(e.g. in Qt you can register sockets with the event loop and ask to be
notified when they're readable/writable). The downside here is that it
would require significant restructuring of the kernel, and I have no idea
what the public API would look like.

Right now I think the real question is, how do users *want* GUI + curio
code to work? What would be a pleasant and usable API? It's not at all
clear given how callback-happy existing toolkits are, but if we knew that
then we could start figuring out how to get there.

It's a minor thing, but I've found myself mostly sticking to using the
context manager forms of the timeout commands anyway. I could see an
argument for making that the One Way to do it, just to simplify things a
bit.

One thing that tripped me up, the ExecutePreprocessor.timeout is set to 60 seconds, which means an exception is thrown if a cell takes longer than that to execute. This is pretty conservative for a preprocessor that is executing arbitrary code. You can change it at the command line:

```
--ExecutePreprocessor.timeout=3600
```

or set a new default in your ipython_nbcnonvert_config.py:

```
c = get_config()

I just spent several hours working around this issue on Ubuntu 13.04.  I want my app to use the keyring to store database passwords securely, so I used keyring with the default implementation.  Imagine my surprise when I decided to see what backend it was using and I see PlainText!  

FWIW, I would strongly recommend the default option to be to throw an error when only insecure backends are available and, if the developer wants to allow insecure backends, make that be an extra hoop to jump through.  Explicit is better than implicit.

```
#!python

keyring.allow_insecure_backend = True
...
```

I'm currently restricting usage of any backends with priority < 1, but don't think that's a great solution since priority is arbitrary.  I'd prefer to see a property like:

```
#!python

if not backend.is_secure:
    raise ...
```

Note that the Arabic characters U+0601 etc. are defined by the unicode standard as exceptions to usual rule that Cf characters are invisible:

* *Unlike most other format control characters, however, they should be rendered with a visible glyph, even in circumstances where no suitable digit or sequence of digits follows them in logical order.* — Unicode Standard v.6.2.0, Section 8.2 - Arabic (p.256)

In contrast, e.g. U+200E is a [left-to-right mark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Left-to-right_mark), and in my understanding is defined as an invisible formatting character that controls the direction of the text.   Some terminals may give it a nonzero width (although the MacOS Terminal with the default font gives it zero width on my machine), but that seems like a bug in the terminal (or the font); it seems like it is better to return what the Unicode standard says rather than mimicking a particular buggy implementation.

```
class AppConfig(RequiredConfigMixin):
    required_config = ConfigOptions()
    required_config.add_option('debug', parser=bool)
    ...

    def __init__(self, config):
        self.config = config.with_options(self)

    def __getitem__(self, key):
        return self.config(key)
```

Then in your app initialization, you'd create the ConfigManager and then your AppConfig instance. Abusing getitem notation, we could have something like this:

```
def get_app():
    config = ConfigManager([
            ConfigOSEnv(),
            ConfigIniEnv([
                os.environ.get('FOO_INI'),
                '~/.foo.ini',
                '/etc/foo.ini'
            ]),
        ])
    app_config = AppConfig(config)

    debug = app_config['debug']
```

Or something like that.

This is helpful in certain situations. You wouldn't use this in conjunction with Django settings, but you might use it in a Flask app or a web.py app.

GraphViz is distributed separately, and is available in package managers for the most widely used operating systems. As far as I can tell, it is used as an external program by the Python package `graphviz`, so no need to compile any bindings. Therefore no need to install it as part of the `graphviz` package.

[PEP 470 -- Removing External Hosting Support on PyPI](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0470/) removed external downloads from PyPI. Fetching by default a download from outside PyPI violates this condition, because it silently performs a download external to PyPI (thus, it is also a security concern). So, `pip install graphviz` should not download GraphViz, unless the user explicitly requests so.

An optional external download could be done with a command line option to `setup.py`, as done in the package `dd`, described [here](https://github.com/johnyf/dd/tree/e5aa95bf118db18699d4f0c297e0f9dfc68243a7#dd-fetching-cudd), and implemented with [this script](https://github.com/johnyf/dd/blob/e5aa95bf118db18699d4f0c297e0f9dfc68243a7/download.py). However, such a download seems unnecessary and a mix of concerns, since no bindings to GraphViz need be compiled.

Perhaps a more dependable solution is to include GraphViz within a wheel for `graphviz`. This conforms to PEP 470, since no external download occurs. However, the main effect will be to unnecessarily increase the wheel's size, and most users will already have GraphViz installed from their OS's package manager.

Also, please remember to include the license of GraphViz if it is part of the distribution of the `graphviz` package.

@JBraumueller You probably want to avoid fracturing before the fillet operation. Both `shape` and `merged` will be fractured if you don't specify otherwise. To do so you have to set the `max_points` argument in [`fast_boolean`](https://gdspy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/geometry.html#gdspy.fast_boolean) to something sufficiently large (or zero to disable checking completely).

In that case you probably don't need to manually merge the result from the first operation:

```python
shape = gdspy.fast_boolean(Rectangle, Circle, 'not', max_points=0)
shape.fillet(20, 100)
```

Note that if the circle is inside the rectangle (`shape` has a hole) `fillet` is probably give the wrong result because of the connection between the hole and the outside boundary. In that case, you'll probably want to run `fillet` before the boolean operation.

My point is mock is going to tell you very little about whether you broke Juniper or not (the thing that matters is the timing and the device's CLI behavior i.e. devices behaving differently on the CLI than we expected). 

Mock would not have helped in a meaningful way on your proposed timing changes (if anything it would probably deceive you i.e. give you a false sense you weren't breaking things when you were).

Paramiko is much easier to to mock than Netmiko. Linux is much more uniform in its behavior and timing is much less of an issue. Sure we can mock it, but if we spend 100 hours on it and it doesn't provide us significant, useful information, then that is not an improvement (and maintaining mocked behavior is a lot of work also i.e. it is not just N hours of one-time work).

Mock or no-mock, you will have a very hard time making meaningful Netmiko changes without access to network devices (once again because device timing and device behavior is so important). Virtual network devices are pretty easy to obtain; you can also use labs.networktocode.com to spin-up network devices on demand for a pretty nominal cost.

Thanks for the feedback and bringing it up

The chosen naming convention was mostly based on what I found intuitive wrt the rcsb PDB "definition" column -- I didn't think about VMD at all when I chose those column names.

I agree with you that this may be confusing/inconvenient for a VMD user though ... Since this is a dev tool, I am

> record_name vs atom

I wouldn't change that to 'atom', since the `record_name` column could also store HETATM and ANISOU entries, for instance.

> atom_number vs index 

I'd stick with atom_number here, because it is more clear what this refers to. E.g., in the context of pandas, `index` could also mean DataFrame index

> atom_name vs name

again, I find the `atom` prefix more clear, since 'name' is more ambiguous (atom vs residue vs molecule name)

> alt_loc vs altLoc  

Not sure, for some reason I don't like camelcase (except for Python classes). "Don't like" is actually a bit too strong: I prefer smallercase whenever possible, because I don't have to remember which letter to capitalize (okay, but there's an underscore one might say ...).

> residue_name vs resname 

No strong preference here; maybe residue_name is a bit (too) verbose!? What's confusing though is that VMD uses `resname` instead of e.g., `resName` like it does for altLoc

> x_coord, y_coord, z_coord vs x, y, z

That's one thing that could be shortened. `_coord` is a bit too verbose maybe. On the other hand, it's more clear what this refers to (thinking of math variables)

> b_factor vs beta

I'd go with b_factor or temp_factor here; beta is too ambiguos imho (again, may sound like a generic variable name)

> element_symbol vs element

that could definitely be shortened to just element; I don't see any reason to make it more verbose?!

Although I actually like the current column names, i agree that it's maybe a bit annoying if they are different from the PDB specs. I think changing them to the PDB spec column names sounds reasonable. Another thing to think about other data formats (SDF, MOL2, ...). I.e., what's more useful, having column names consistent across different dataformats or using a column name that is specific to each data format. E.g., in MOL2, there's "atom_id & atom_name" vs "serial & name" in PDB. 

I am slightly leaning towards using the official "field names" from each format's spec though ...

Thanks for looking into this so quickly. It seems like requests uses the timeout functionality from urllib3, which uses the default Python socket timeout. As far as I can tell, there is no default Python socket timeout and the actual timeout depends on the socket connection timeout on the underlying platform.

I did a bit of digging in Requests and found that [it also accepts a chunk size of None](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/api/#requests.Response.iter_content), which seems like it might be exactly what we want:
> chunk_size must be of type int or None. A value of None will function differently depending on the value of stream. stream=True will read data as it arrives in whatever size the chunks are received. If stream=False, data is returned as a single chunk.

I've made this change to one of my clients to see if this works as expected; I'll post here with an update soon. The streams I'm working with aren't very busy, so it would be good to hear from someone who has a busier stream on what, if any, performance impact this will have versus a larger chunk size.

uWSGI tries to (ab)use the Copy On Write semantics of the fork() call whenever possible. By default it will fork after having loaded your applications to share as much of their memory as possible. Beware as there is an older options named lazy that is way more invasive and highly discouraged (it is still here only for backward compatibility)

@reinecke 

At the very start, I try to validate a data like this: 

```python
subschema = schema['definition...']['...']
flex.validate(subschema, data)
```
As a result, the program will complain.

After I read the source code, I found that there is a parameter named `context`. It refer to the original swagger schema.
```
flex.validate(subschema, data, context=schema)
```

I don't know if you are having the same problem as mine.

As long as I know, there's no rule for matching priority in swagger.
It's natural that exact matching should have higher priority over regular expression matching.

I think I'd be more inclined to do this using a method rather than overloading indexed access (square brackets). Maybe `Shapes.get_by_name(shape_name, default=KeyError)`.

The behavior would be:

* if shape with matching name is found, the first match is returned (no guarantee they have unique names, so there could be more than one match.)
* if no match and `default` is an instance of `Exception`, that exception is raised.
* if no match and `default` is set to a value (e.g. `None` or `my_other_shape`), that default value is returned.

Another benefit is it allows room for other search methods, in particular `Shapes.get_by_id(shape_id, default=KeyError)`, which I happen to need myself, which typically increases the chances of a successful implementation and merge :)

I assume you store your units to a variable - the units inside that variable will not be updated (position, health, energy, orders too I suppose).
Instead, you have to use `self.units` to update your tracked units.

If you only track one units, e.g. `self.scout_scv` then you need to do this each step:
`self.scout_scv = self.units.find_by_tag(self.scout_scv.tag)`
Note: self.scout_scv will be `None` if it died.

If you track multiple units, you can store them in a set (fastest), list or dict:
`self.my_army_tags = self.units(MARINE).tags`
 Then each step you have to do:
`my_army = self.units.tags_in(self.my_army_tags)` 
Dead units will not show up in `my_army`. So `len(my_army)` may be less than `len(self.my_army_tags)`.

Let's start with the exception forwarding:

Do you know a case of when forwarding callback exceptions to the main exception context would *not* be a good idea? Aside of the error callback, all other callbacks are only called from within `glfwPollEvents` and `glfwWaitEvents`, so if a user wishes to ignore callback exceptions, I think:
```
try:
    glfw.poll_events()
except:
    pass
```
would be the pythonic way to do it in user code, and it would already show the user that ignoring these exceptions might not be a good idea.

Thread-safety is no issue here, as the relevant GLFW functions are [not thread-safe](http://www.glfw.org/docs/latest/intro_guide.html#thread_safety) anyway.

I'd suggest the following behavior:
If an uncaught exception causes a callback to exit, the exception is re-raised from the current PyGLFW function. The stack trace is not printed.
If the callback was called from inside `glfw.poll_events` or `glfw.wait_events`, execution of other callbacks is disabled for this call of the function (and of course re-enabled for future calls of the function).

This leads to the typical behavior of executions: prevent code execution and jump to the next `except` or quit the program. This would also mean that there can only be one "buffered" exception at a time. I think this would cause the least surprise to a Python developer.

What are your thoughts about this suggested behavior?
___________________________

For optional features like error checking, I think the approach of using module level constants as done in PyOpenGL is quite good.
```
import glfw
glfw.ERROR_REPORTING = False
```
This code would opt-out of the automatic propagation of GLFW errors to Python exceptions. I guess this would be the default error callback and setting the error callback to `None` would actually re-install this error-to-exception callback?

Alternatively, an explicit `glfw.set_error_callback(None)` could disable the default?

How large is the largest tau-value you are calculating and plotting, compared to the total length of the time-series? A conservative rule might be tau_max = length/4. So if you have 1000 points collected at 1s interval the largest tau you should reasonable hope to extract an ADEV at is about tau=250s.
The overlapping allan-deviation is a bit better in this respect than the standard ADEV, and the TOTDEV in particular is designed to work around this limitation (it copies and reflects your original data to create a longer ('syntehtic') input dataset). That approach is valid if your clock really is a noise process and copying/reflecting the data produces more realistic data.

Error-bars are not working out-of-the-box correctly currently in allantools. There are examples on how to properly calculate them with first a power-law-noise estimator and then greenhall_edf(). If the errorbars work correctly you should get larger and larger errorbars at large tau - so the last points you see might not be anomalous they just have a very large confidence interval.

This custom exception is replacing the normal error you would encounter in python 3.5:
`SyntaxError: Missing parentheses in call to 'print'`

@mcfletch six doesn't intentionally import it from pies. Pies places a module in site-packages which shadows the standard library causing all kinds of awful horrors. This was found to be a problem with a couple of non-six compatibility libraries in https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/issues/253. Six just works because it's explicit and doesn't try to do magic to your python environment.

This should also be fixed in newer versions of urllib3 and requests. If it isn't, please post the traceback and version information so we can further guard against this.

As you wrote before it looks like that your delete_master() trigger doesn't work for some reason and I need to see your model to figure out what's so special about it.

> I have last question: Why you have an after insert trigger, why not only a before insert which will insert the row and return NULL ? Is it for safety ?
This is because some ORMs (e.g. Django and maybe others, I don't remember right now for sure) are waiting for the result from the database and returning NULL will break them. Interestingly, if I don't use `django-apscheduler` jobstore, i.e., just use memory apscheduler, I can partially solve this by adding import of model classes in the callable of the job. 

I mean, instead of doing:

```
from backEnd.models import TPlanifiedJobs
from backEnd.serializers import TPlanifiedJobsSerializer
from backEnd import tcpdmanage

def executionJob(idProcessPlan, username):
    logger.debug("Starting Execution Job...")
    planifyJobs=TPlanifiedJobs.objects.get(id=idProcessPlan)  # here the exception
    ...
```

I do:

```
def executionJob(idProcessPlan, username):
    from backEnd.models import TPlanifiedJobs
    from backEnd.serializers import TPlanifiedJobsSerializer
    from backEnd import tcpdmanage
    logger.debug("Starting Execution Job...")
    planifyJobs=TPlanifiedJobs.objects.get(id=idProcessPlan)  # here the exception
    ...

```

And the job gets executed. I think by importing the module Django reestablish the connection with DB and then enable the connection for APScheduler again.

By the way, in order to avoid waiting for one day to test the scheduler's activity, you can reproduce by:

0. Create a scheduler without `djang-apscheduler` jobstore.
1. Planify a job in 5 minutes. In the job to execute, add some DB consult, like find by id.
2. Stop mysql/mariadb service. (`systemctl stop mariadb`, etc)
3. Start mysql/mariadb again.
4. Wait till the moment of execution. 

You can see the same error in log.

But, if we don't specify the jobstore persistency, we have no way to track jobs if apache/mariadb/the machine restarts.

Note: as of orderedmultidict v0.7.8, omdict objects can no longer be passed to
the `data=` argument with Requests. That is, this code

```python
>>> import requests
>>> import orderedmultidict
>>> o = orderedmultidict.omdict([('a', 'a'), ('b', 'b')])
>>> requests.post('http://httpbin.org/post', data=o).json().get('form')
```

will raise `TypeError: must be string or buffer, not set` in v0.7.8. No such
exception is raised in v0.7.7.

In v0.7.8, omdict switched to subclass `collections.MutableMapping` instead of
v0.7.7's `dict`. As learned, subclassing `dict` is a bad idea due to Python's
'magic' methods of built-in containers.


Zooming out, the fact that Requests raises an exception when `data=` is passed
an omdict object is a bug/oversight in Requests. Dictionary-like objects
shouldn't have to subclass dict itself to be a valid `data=` parameter. I
created a ticket to resolve this.

  https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/3649

Until resolved, to pass an omdict object as the `data=` parameter, either create
a native from the omdict object

```python
>>> o = orderedmultidict.omdict([('a', 'a'), ('b', 'b')])
>>> requests.post('http://httpbin.org/post', data=dict(o)).json().get('form')
```

or `urlencode()` it directly

```python
>>> from urllib import urlencode
>>> o = orderedmultidict.omdict([('a', 'a'), ('b', 'b')])
>>> requests.post('http://httpbin.org/post', data=urlencode(o)).json().get('form')
```

from asyncio import Queue

Source: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19417412/ffmpeg-concatenation-filters-stream-specifier-0-in-filtergraph-matches-no-st

> Although the error message is not very user friendly you need to tell the concat filter the number of output video streams. The default is 1 for video and 0 for audio, so it's usually a good idea to be explicit:
> ```bash
> $ ffmpeg -i OHIn.wav -i OHOut.wav -filter_complex \
>  '[0:0] [1:0] concat=n=2:v=0:a=1 [a]' -map '[a]' output.wav
> ```

Hmm, actually it might be a little more non-trivial. Take the following for example: ``` ffmpeg.view([ (ffmpeg .input('in1.mp4') .output('out1.mp4') ), (ffmpeg .input('in2.mp4') .output('out2.mp4') ), (ffmpeg .input('in3.mp4') .hflip() .output('out3.mp4') ), (ffmpeg .input('in3.mp4') .vflip() .output('out4.mp4') ), (ffmpeg .input('in5.mp4') .hflip() .split()[0] .output('out5.mp4') ), ]) ``` This has four connected components, two of which are passthroughs and two of which use filters. For fully connected graphs, this is a non-issue, so it seems to be an issue if one or more passthroughs is used _and_ multiple connected components are present. There are two solutions that I know of: - Inject a dummy filter (e.g. `split=n=1`) into passthroughs so that they become part of the `-filter_complex` and thus get a stream number assigned so that `-map` can be used. Probably the simplest way to go. - Detect the individual connected components and output each passthrough individually, and combine any that use filters. The picture above would be broken up as follows: The two passthroughs are highlighted in orange and the two filter-based components are combined in the purple box. This is certainly more complicated but it gets rid of the need for an extra dummy filter. I'm not sure if a dummy filter affects performance in any way, so it's almost certainly best to do it the simple way first and then optimize later. Good to know that there's a way to do it in a more ideal way if needed though.

I haven't come across this myself, but the root of the cause is this:

```
DEBUG:requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:https://*****.com:443 "POST /Autodiscover/Autodiscover.xml HTTP/1.1" 400 None
```

We try to POST an autodiscover request (an XML document describing the information we want to retrieve) for ***@***.com to `https://*****.com/Autodiscover/Autodiscover.xml`. Let's assume this is where you expected autodiscover requests to land (otherwise there's a bug in our autodiscover algorithm). This server is returning HTTP 400 (Bad Request). Additionally, it's sending a bogus response body which the the `requests` module doesn't like, so `requests` throws `ChunkedEncodingError`, but that's actually a bit irrelevant here because the interesting part is the HTTP 400.

I have no idea why your server would throw HTTP 400 on this request. Maybe it's misconfigured, maybe it's not the right server to handle this request, maybe we're not trying hard enough to look like a Microsoft client.

> This signal is triggered just after a revision is saved to the database. It receives the following keyword arguments:

Well as a migration path, could I suggestion adding a new `verify` keyword parameter to the `BaseConnection.__init__()` with a default of true. This parameter should be passed to the requests call. If users experience the `InsecurePlatformException` and are unable to install the required dependencies they are then able to revert to the default (insecure) behaviour--but in full knowledge that they are proceeding with an insecure connection.

An alternative suggestion (but this is a more radical jigging of the architecture of the library) is this: Currently the a requests session object is set up within the `ebaysdk` library. A more object-oriented approach would be to accept a requests session object as a *parameter*. Then the library users can set up session options (proxies, SSL certificate verification, authentication) to their own requirements, and the `ebaysdk` code (especially initialisation) is considerably simplified.

The in-source documentation is insufficient when you're readme points to the "Complete" documentation on readthedocs. The docs on read the docs mentions that you can use the login_required decorator, but contains no mention that the decorator, may be disabled by a single unrelated config setting. Not disabled as in we'll block access, but disabled as in: When you disable login, we disable the security features you're trying to gain by requiring users to be authenticated. I clicked through to read the source code on a whim, if I hadn't I'd never had known I can disable security features unintentionally....

If that's really the design you guys have chosen, wtf? 

It's fine to make the assumption, but it's not okay to name it LOGIN_DISABLED, when what it does is disable security features. If the reasoning that this is wrong isn't clear. I can cite plenty of standards or implementations that, when disabling the ability to login, doesn't also disable the requirement that a user is logged in before accessing restricted sections. 

>  I don't see why that suddenly makes login_required not suitable for the things it's designed for.

1. The login_required decorator is likely being used as access control.
2. Wanting to prevent users from being able to login is a valid, and common use case (you even support that idea with "fresh sessions".
3. If I disable login, without reading the source. Instead of increasing security by prohibiting users from logging in. What I'm actually doing is disabling ALL security features.

If you're fine with a security feature that you can completely disable. That's fine too, just document it correctly so no one gets screwed, and I'll just write my own authorization manager.

Please ask questions on [Stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flask-login?sort=votes).

User loader is called every request because users can be deleted and shouldn't rely only on the session to stay logged in. It sounds like you are trying to directly send an email using MailChimp to someone on demand? If this is a correct interpretation of your request, that is not really generally available through the API, if at all. MailChimp is primarily for managing email marketing, newsletters, and other similar volume email needs, where a single template will be sent to a number of users.

It is possible to send a single email to a single person using this system, but will be cumbersome. You will need to create a new campaign that contains the email that you would like to send, and create an email list for that campaign to use that contains the individual that you would like to receive the email. You would then need to schedule the email to go out as a one time send. The various endpoints have documentation on their use that you can find at the MailChimp developer portal. If you look at the source code for any endpoint, we have a link directly to the documentation location for that endpoint, as well as a link to the top level of the developer documentation included in the README files.

Let us know if this is what you were talking about, or if you find a different way of doing this, or more importantly, if I have misinterpreted your question.

Task arguments can be any valid Python type and will only be serialized with the configured [task serializer](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/calling.html#serializers) when sent to between Celery clients and workers.

The goal of this package is to use the task and worker event state to conduct monitoring, which in turn provides its values verbatim without serialization -- by design. So in other words we're erring on the side of correctness instead of convenience. There is also an additional operational risk of converting the arguments to JSON during storing that could lead to monitoring race conditions if for example the conversion to JSON fails and prevents updating the task state in the database.

There are a few options to get what you want nevertheless (with the caveat that you'd be on your own):

- subclass `django_celery_monitor.camera.Camera`, override the `update_task` method (and calling the parent `update_task` method first to continue the usual functionality) and store the arguments in JSON (or whatever form is convenient for you) in a separate datastorage (e.g. a separate data model)
- we add a Django signal to this package (e.g. `celery_task_monitored`) so you can do option 1 without subclassing, the rest stays the same
- post-process task state updates using Django's `post_save` signal and convert the arguments to the format you require, and store it in a separate table

@msabramo Hey, how's it going?

So, Requests has been dealing with this a bit recently, as @graingert noted. The relevant requests issues are: kennethreitz/requests#3734, kennethreitz/requests#3735, kennethreitz/requests#3713, shazow/urllib3#1080, and shazow/urllib3#1089. As you can see by the length of that list, which is not even entirely exhaustive, we've spent a lot of time trying to thread the needle of the kind of scheme overloading that is happening here.

The specific version of the problem, at least as Requests sees it, is this: Requests knows how the HTTP/HTTPS URL schemes work, and assumes it does not understand how anything else works. Requests has a very strong view of this, meaning that for a scheme it does not understand it won't do *anything* to the URL, including add the `params` parameters to it. This is intended to allow extremely different URLs such as `file://` to get through unmolested.

*However*, there have been a few cases where users have wanted requests to treat the URL like it's HTTP, even though it isn't. In this case, due to a lazily-coded check in Requests, it has been possible to get this behaviour for schemes that *begin* with `http` and then go on to do something wacky. This causes us an enormous number of problems because users generally only want us to do a subset of the things we know how to do to URLs: the most recent examples are all related to unix sockets and indicate that those users want us to do query parameters, but not IDNA. Because we broke a bunch of people with our IDNA change, we reverted it in the 2.12 series.

We've committed to continue to support this behaviour in the 2.x release series. However, it has caused us an enormous amount of problems, because there is no consistency over what exactly users want. The only situation where the Requests team is safe to keep evolving our URL handling if is we touch *only* HTTP schemed URLs, so the only thing we're committing to for the future 3.0 release is to work with *exactly* `http` and `https` schemes only. This means that the 3.0 release of Requests may not work as you expect with a `http+unix` or similar scheme URL.

However, if we do that we'll be adding the ability for those that provide transport adapters to also provide hooks for URL handling, such that the logic currently done in `prepare_url` can be overridden in a scheme-appropriate way. This would allow requests-unixsocket to tell requests to treat a scheme in a specific way (including adding the `params` to it appropriately).

The TL;DR of all this is that any of the approaches here will work well. The approach of using `http://` plus a dummy domain will work well in 3.0 as well, but `http+whatever` will potentially require more work for the 3.0 series.

Here's an example that will demonstrate this nicely:

```
import asyncio
import asyncio_redis


CHANNEL = 'my_channel'

async def listen(sub, sub_name):
    for i in range(10):
        print('{}: {}'.format(sub_name, await sub.next_published()))

async def publish_loop(pub_c):
    for i in range(10):
        await pub_c.publish(CHANNEL, 'message {}'.format(i))

async def build_pubsub(host='redis', port=6379):
    sub_c1 = await asyncio_redis.connection.Connection.create(host, port)
    sub_c2 = await asyncio_redis.connection.Connection.create(host, port)
    sub1 = await sub_c1.start_subscribe()
    sub2 = await sub_c2.start_subscribe()
    pub_c = await asyncio_redis.connection.Connection.create(host, port)
    return pub_c, sub1, sub2

async def test_pubsub():
    pub_c, sub_good, sub_bad = await build_pubsub()
    
    # Subscribe to channels
    await sub_good.subscribe([CHANNEL])
    await sub_bad.subscribe([])
    await sub_bad.subscribe([CHANNEL])
    
    # Start listeners
    asyncio.ensure_future(listen(sub_good, 'sub_good'))
    asyncio.ensure_future(listen(sub_bad, 'sub_bad'))
    
    # Publish messages
    await publish_loop(pub_c)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(test_pubsub())
```

' + 'return: ' + _ret + '
' + 'error: ' + _err params = yaml_doc.get('input', []) for i in params: _name = i.get('name') _desc = i.get('desc') _required = i.get('required', False) _type = i.get('type', 'string') _location = i.get('location', 'form') field = coreapi.Field( name=_name, location=_location, required=_required, description=_desc, type=_type ) fields.append(field) else: _method_desc = a[0] fields += self.get_serializer_fields(path, method) fields += self.get_pagination_fields(path, method) fields += self.get_filter_fields(path, method) manual_fields = self.get_manual_fields(path, method) fields = self.update_fields(fields, manual_fields) if fields and any([field.location in ('form', 'body') for field in fields]): encoding = self.get_encoding(path, method) else: encoding = None if base_url and path.startswith('/'): path = path[1:] return coreapi.Link( url=urlparse.urljoin(base_url, path), action=method.lower(), encoding=encoding, fields=fields, description=_method_desc ) schema_view = get_swagger_view(title='Product Testing API') ``` Your urls.py file references it like: ```python from .schema_view import schema_view urlpatterns = [ url(r'^v1/api/', include([ url(r'^doc/', schema_view), ])), ``` No the `sad` part is that you have to override all your APIView or ModelViewSet with ```python schema = CustomSchema() ``` as described in the 3.7.7 release : http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/schemas/#per-view-schema-customisation @m-haziq Hope it will help for making the migration in your README file ;)

My main motivation to write these scripts was, that the original perl version of AWS CloudWatchMonitoringScripts I used (version 1.1.0) were unable to handle the version 4 authentication scheme (http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/sig-v4-authenticating-requests.html) of new AWS regions (such as Frankfurt). This has changed with their version 1.2 released on 2015-02-25.

I offered my version to AWS, but they decided to update their Perl version instead.

Without doing a scientific analysis, it seem that my version is about 30% faster and has only very little dependencies (boto). I'm using my version on several t2.micro instances and a single call to `mon-put-instance-stats.py --mem-util --disk-space-util --disk-path /` takes about 140 ms. I couldn't measure the CPU load for typical execution interval (once every minute).

I'm not using the Perl version anymore, because of its heavy dependency footprint.

When `HTMLTokenizer` tokenize text given to it, it unescape all html entities and then construct nodes. https://github.com/html5lib/html5lib-python/blob/master/html5lib/tokenizer.py#L253 The reason why this unescaped entities are rendered properly as escaped is becuase `HTMLSerializer` escapes them again when `token["type"]` is either `"Characters"` or `"SpaceCharacters"` https://github.com/html5lib/html5lib-python/blob/master/html5lib/serializer/htmlserializer.py#L223 But in `bleach` code, `replace_nodes` reconstruct `Element` from its text. https://github.com/mozilla/bleach/blob/master/bleach/__init__.py#L149 But right now we don't care whether type of a token containing
is "Character" or not. ``` original data <br> example.com after tokenizing |
example.com | | type="Characters"| after replace_nodes |
|| example.com | | |type="EmptyTag"| type="StartTag" |type=Characters|type="EndTag"| ``` So yeah, it seems like `replace_nodes` is broken-as-designed. We should not construct new `Element` from giving whole node.text into `parseFragment` but should deal with only the url part in the node. Having spent the morning testing out the implementation I found it created a confusing layer of abstraction, removing much of the usefulness of having a string directly on *Match instances.  It forced me to add two lookup tables to translate string -> int and int -> string.  Calls to those lookups ended up being peppered around any use of the *Match models. While testing out the choices implementation I also took another look at the validation of matcher names, particularly after our discussion around configuring them in settings, and concluded we shouldn't validate them either.  It makes the tests simpler (a matcher name is just a string for tests that don't care about the matcher used) and avoids the need to use any configuration. > If someone sees a clean solution that would add it only to configurable traits, that would be fine, too.

Part of the issue here is that there are two different ways right now to specify default values for TraitTypes, and they're logically owned by different objects:

1. Registering a dynamic default method on a `HasTraits` subclass, in which case it's the **traited** object that owns the default behavior.

2. Passing `default_value` to an instance of `TraitType`, in which case it's the **trait itself** that logically owns the defaulting behavior.

The question, I think, is whether creating a default from an environment variable should be thought of as shorthand for creating a dynamic default method (in which case the logic for defining the default should be owned by `HasTraits` or `MetaHasTraits`), or whether it should be thought of as a slightly fancier way of specifying `default_value`, in which case the logic for defining the default should be owned by `TraitType`.

If we go with the "envvar default is owned by `TraitType`" model (which is what I've implemented here), then I don't think it makes sense to restrict this behavior to Configurables, since `TraitType` doesn't know anything about configuration.

If we decide that this functionality should be owned by `HasTraits`, then we could plausibly push it down to `Configurable` instead.

"all the suitable date and date-time representations" There's a very
standard model in Python for datetimes and that's the `datetime.datetime`
class. On it there is a method strftime which lets you format time in
whatever display standard you want. So supplying the datetime in
`datetime.datetime`
format would make life easy for people wanting to represent the datetimes
in whichever format they want. Note that this is the approach that Django
takes, among many other packages, when exposing datetime fields.

When you input text, the previous text content is cleared by design. With date/dateTime and such fields the input can't be made blank. If you can manually input a date to the field, you may still try to use inputs such as `04112018`, `04/11/2018 `and `04-11-2018`. The idea is to fill full the perfect short form which matches the input type (so you wouldn't use `4` as a date, but `04`).

The biggest yet-to-be-resolved issue with the design of the CLI is: Do we deviate from 1:1 parity with the API to provide higher-level functions? 

* For the sake of docs and consistency with the SDK interfaces, we say *No*
* For the sake of higher level functionality and improved interfaces ( #36, #37, #39, #43, #44, #46, #49 ) we seem to be suggesting *Yes*

Maybe we need to define what is and what is not within the scope of this module in terms of how far we can deviate from the API-level interface. For instance, the batch geocoding and high-level datasets API implementations are awesome but they could also be broken out as separate tools (even sharing the same "mapbox" namespace through the use of click plugins).

@sbma44 @dnomadb this PR is ready for a review. There are more CLI features than I intended in this, so I'll try to summarize the intent again.

1. `--forward` and `--reverse` are a boolean option pair. Exclusive. No values for this option. Forward is the default.
2. A round trip (using jq for JSON wrangling) goes like this:

```bash
$ export MapboxAccessToken=MY_TOKEN
$ mapbaux geocode "1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC" | \
> jq '.features[0].center' -c | \
> mapbaux geocode --reverse | \
> jq '.features[0].place_name'
"1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, 20006, District of Columbia, United States"
```

3. The `query` argument for the commands can accept stdin (the default), a filename, or a positional argument. Here's a stdin example using just one query, but you can send any number of queries on stdin.

```bash
$ cat << EOF | mapbaux geocode | jq '.features[] | .center' -c
> "1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC"
> EOF
[-77.036698,38.897102]
[-77.020749,38.892999]
[-76.982024,38.878977]
[-122.642074,47.575456]
[-76.634388,39.30307]
```

Here's an example of passing the query as a positional argument.

```bash
$ mapbaux geocode "1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC" | jq '.features[] | .center' -c
[-77.036698,38.897102]
[-77.020749,38.892999]
[-76.982024,38.878977]
[-122.642074,47.575456]
[-76.634388,39.30307]
```

4. The format for coordinate queries accepts `[lng, lat]`, `lng, lat`, or `lng lat`. Note that you need a leading space before the minus sign because of an [argparse defect](https://bugs.python.org/issue9334). Perhaps separate `--lat` and `--lng` options taking numerical values would be a better complement to file input for reverse geocoding?

```bash
$ mapbaux geocode " -77.0366 38.897" --reverse | jq '.features[] | .place_name' -c
"1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, 20006, District of Columbia, United States"
"Washington, 20006, District of Columbia, United States"
"20006, District of Columbia, United States"
"District of Columbia, United States"
"United States"
```

One thing: it reads the pipfile in each time and uses the contents as a
cache key. So only issue is if you mutate the pipfile, don’t write it to
disk, and also don’t clear the pipfile cache.

contents as cache key.

What's the use case? Support for it is actually provided by distutils itself, not setuptools.

I much prefer the simple asserts (with the pytest 'rewrite asserts' functionality). It means that tests need not be derived from any base class and that the tracebacks are much clearer about the location of the issue (often when using a framework with `assertFalse`, the traceback appears rooted in that method and not where the assertion was made).

A better change would be to remove all of the `.assertSomething` methods in favor of direct asserts.

After reviewing the legacy [distutils](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/distutils.html), I would guess that it was an attempt to maintain compatibility with the `distutils`'s [`MANIFEST` logic](https://docs.python.org/3.6/distutils/sourcedist.html#manifest-related-options):

> - if the manifest file (`MANIFEST` by default) exists and the first line does not have a comment indicating it is generated from `MANIFEST.in`, then it is used as is, unaltered

However this makes sense in `distutils`, where the `MANIFEST` is either automatically generated or authored, and not in `setuptools` where `.egg-info/SOURCES.txt` is always generated.

Not a silly idea at all. My goal was to be minimally invasive and provide a compatibility shim that would ease the transition and guide its users to the same conclusion (just use pip directly). I was particularly concerned about the bootstrap scenario, which invokes ez_setup.py through its API. They bundle ez_setup.py in their own bootstrap script. But now that you say it, it's probably the case that their bootstrap will fail at the implicit assumption that pip is installed, in which case it's no help.

It seems to me the options are to provide an ez_setup.py script that tries to do the right thing or to break it down and have it do nothing but raise an error message that it's no longer supported. > ./env/bin/pip freeze --all

+        reqs = []

```
#!/bin/bash

I've hit a bit of a wall and would appreciate some input. The attached figure illustrates my thinking. It has four lines. The question is how to instruct ```hough_line_peak``` to interpret those lines. There are a few different options:

1) interpret each line separately. An ordered list of peaks then would return (3,2,4,1).

2) Interpret lines 1&2 as a single line. The list of peaks is now (1+2, 3, 4). This takes place during the filtering operation.

3)  Interpret all lines separately, but suppress peaks within 30px of a larger peak. Now the returned list is (3,4). This takes place in the neighbourhood suppression.

4) Interpret lines 1&2 as a single line and suppress peaks within 30px of a large one. This returns (1+2, 4).

Note that the filtering range is unrelated to the suppression neighbourhood. So this will require two new arguments to define the filtering range. 
The neighbourhood suppression is done, but I don't know how to perform the filtering to achieve schemes 2 and 4. The current scheme uses a local max filter. This effectively suppresses all minor peaks in a neighbourhood so the intensity from 1 does not contribute to that from 2. 

Any ideas? Is this at all useful? Am I wasting my time?

The api looks good. The two advanced use cases I've thought of can be addressed with it. Those are:
- filtering the AST before building the query so that private records fields cannot be used by user queries. This is possible by using a dedicated Query Enhancer.
- use different Query Enhancers depending on the type of record. This is possible by giving the type as a parameter to the `Query` constructor or by calling `build` and enabling the enhancers when needed.

You can solve this by using the ``@shared_task`` decorator + the connect to the ``import_modules`` celery signal. See https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-celery/blob/master/invenio_celery/ext.py#L86-L91

That's correct: `class` is a reserved word in Python, and since patsy allows arbitrary Python code inside formulas, Python's regular reserved-word rules apply to formulas too.

The workaround is to use patsy's [quoting operator, `Q`](https://patsy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/builtins-reference.html?highlight=quote#patsy.builtins.Q). Like:

```python
# doesn't work:
dmatrix("age + class", data=...)

# does work:
dmatrix("y ~ age + q('class')", data=...)
```

I suppose we could hack around this by detecting reserved words that can't possibly appear inside an expression (e.g. `class`, `for`, `def`, ...), and "autoquoting" them, but I don't anticipate having time to work on this anytime soon.

This averaging across potentially different distributions and underlying processes is not possible by statistical means. I strongly discourage to provide a functionality like this because people tend to “press buttons” without thinking and blame the stupid results on the button providers.

> Apart from the format, it seems that what we are addressing here is not that much about parameters, but more about how to build reusable patterns of computations

Yes ! 

We have two principles for writing formulas :

**Ad-hoc code**
python classes are responsible for computations by defining ad-hoc code (e.g. manipulating dates then returning a vector, that's what python is good at) for complicated and non-reusable computations.

**Reusable code**
a) - python classes should explicitely call a templated operation (bareme, rate multiplication...) if it exists, passing arguments coming from local simulation.calculate() calls.

b) - *python classes are not used anymore* for reusable computations that can reasonably be expressed in YAML (or whatever declarative), which will behind the scenes call the necessary simulation.calculate.

My examples here are halfway between 2a and 2b, but *I'm convinced we should explore the latter*. 

The dramatic drawback of approach a) to me is that your formula parameters are always only half of the story : "Here are some numbers related to `agffc`, but you won't be able to trust them or edit them without diving into the python-numpy code (because it can switch on conditions, modify the base of the bareme, etc.) that you'll find somewhere, hopefully with a similar name". 
If the security of this approach is prefered, since it excludes non-coders, I don't see a reason to keep params in separated files (where the formula's hierarchy is reproduced in some way) except of course for those that are shared.

> There might be some confusion here:
> 
> period_unit:
> NO_UNIT means I can compute the value for any period you asked for
> PERMANENT means I always return the same value independently of the period
> We may want formula that can be computed monthly and yearly
> No computation at instant right now

I think that a *formula* is always applied on a month *or* a year. A *variable* is computed using a formula or using cached input data. A variable may be called on any period (1 year, n years, 1 month, n months) or can be constant (then it is an input variable with no formula). In the end there are 3 cases concerning period granularity :
* a variable is computed using a monthly formula or a monthly cache
* a variable is computed using a yearly formula or a yearly cache
* a variable is constant (implies there is no formula and this is an input variable)

The way a variable uses its formula is the matter of another PR.

@benjello Do you agree on that ?

If we currently have only one tax benefit system that would benefit from it, moreover one that is not the main model for a country, that's a smell of overspecificity. If we can't use this on France, then I don't think many other country packages would use it either.

It is important for contributability to avoid inferences (think of #460), and to make sure that each variable can be edited in a standalone fashion, so that a new contributor may improve or correct it without understanding the whole tax and benefit system. The more definitions are moved in some global, shared state, the more likely errors are made or uncertainty is added on the local level.

I am not in favour of adding support for a feature that seems quite specific. What is the issue with setting the `definition_period` on each variable? If it's the cost of adding, then the regexp given by @fpagnoux seems to do the job. If it's the repetition of the declaration, I understand it might be frustrating when writing. But as always, remember how many times code is read vs how many times it is written. The cost of adding one line that will be autocompleted by one's editor will easily be reimbursed by the time saved by each new contributor one would have to teach about the tax and benefit system specificities.

Another more technical solution would be to extend the Core `Variable`, as described by @sandcha.

In any case, I don't think the Core should provide an option that increases complexity and has such a strong shoot-yourself-in-the-foot potential :wink:

Thanks for the response! I believe AWS also provides the option to specify the credentials in the CLI.

I'm using CarbonBlack from AWS Lambda, which only lets you write to `/tmp`. Lambda is serverless; the only way to upload a credentials file is to bundle it as part of the deploy package, which is obviously undesirable. Instead, you can pass state through environment variables (encrypted with KMS).

So what I was originally trying to do was take the CarbonBlack credentials from the environment variables and write them to disk at runtime in the expected format. But Lambda will not let you write to any of CarbonBlack's supported locations.

One simple solution would be to allow the CarbonBlack user to specify the location of the credentials file. However, in this case, I don't want the credentials to ever even reach the disk.

Hope this helps!

the forking messages with a single hex value (PC) show that we're being inefficient with forking. these forking messages occur when PC becomes symbolic, however we execute a full, quite expensive, forking routine even when there is only 1 SAT value for PC. i've implemented an optimization here: https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/tree/dev-optimize-fork. it's still WIP, but `./solution.py fread sample_input.txt` completes in a matter of minutes with it. i'll be finishing it up and merging it soon!

Ok.Usually, we think different channels represent diffetent features, so we want to normalize these features separately. The main idea is similar with traditional normalization. Batch normalization reducing dimensions except channel-dimension implemented by tensorflow as following:
```
beta = tf.get_variable(name='beta', shape=[n_out], initializer=tf.constant_initializer(0.0)
                               , trainable=True)
gamma = tf.get_variable(name='gamma', shape=[n_out], initializer=tf.random_normal_initializer(1.0, 0.02),
                                trainable=True)
batch_mean, batch_var = tf.nn.moments(x, [0, 1, 2], name='moments')  # data layout : [N H W C]
... other code
normed = tf.nn.batch_normalization(x, mean, var, beta, gamma...)
```

I suspect this will vary significantly with the version of the standard
library that is being used. but the way we write to a std::string to figure
out how many bytes each parameter is going to need on disk is very
inefficient. If we always write n digits per float, we can easily
precompute this and stop using strings.

Making my use of httplib thread-safe fixed the issue. Not sure what I did to cause this error to pop up, but I was tinkering with some related code, so I may have caused it. 

If it helps anyone else, below is the code that I use to get the authentication token. It relies on a dictionary `PRIVATE_KEY` to lookup the service account email and the PEM file (which should probably just be inputs into the function).

```
PRIVATE_KEY = {'my-project' : {'email':'serviceaccount@foo.com', 'key':'my_key.pem'}}
auth_cache = threading.local()
auth_cache_lock = threading.Lock()
def get_auth(project, force = False, scopes = None):
	global auth_cache, auth_cache_lock
	# At some point oauth2client had breaking changes. There are different 
	# copies of this library floating around, so handle whichever default imports.	
	try:
		from oauth2client.client import SignedJwtAssertionCredentials
	except ImportError as e:
		SignedJwtAssertionCredentials = None
		from oauth2client import crypt as oauth_crypt
		from oauth2client.service_account import ServiceAccountCredentials
	if project not in PRIVATE_KEY:
		raise Exception("Cannot connect to %s"%project)
	with auth_cache_lock:
		if not hasattr(auth_cache, 'auths'):
			auth_cache.auths = {}
			
		if not force and project in auth_cache.auths:
			return auth_cache.auths[project]
		f = None
		key = PRIVATE_KEY[project]['key']
		for path in ('updater/', '../updater/', ''):
			pfile = path + key
			try:
				f = file(pfile, 'rb')
			except IOError:
				continue
			else:
				break
		if not f:
			raise Exception("Could not find key file.")
		pem_contents = f.read()
		f.close()
		if scopes == None:
			scopes = DEFAULT_SCOPES
		# The first parameter, service_account_name, is the Email address created 
		# for the Service account. It must be the email address associated with 
		# the key that was created.
		if SignedJwtAssertionCredentials:
			# The old way of doing it.
			credential = SignedJwtAssertionCredentials(
			  PRIVATE_KEY[project]['email'], pem_contents, scope = scopes)
		else:
			# The new way (improvement?)
			signer = oauth_crypt.Signer.from_string(pem_contents)
			credential = ServiceAccountCredentials(
				PRIVATE_KEY[project]['email'], signer, scopes = scopes)
			credential._private_key_pkcs8_pem = pem_contents
		
		http = httplib2.Http()
		http = credential.authorize(http)
		auth_cache.auths[project] = http
		return http

```

There shouldn't be an issue with modules like `readline`.  I'm going to guess that whatever it is, it's because `readline` is included in python as a shared object (.so).  `pp` and thus `ppft` extracts the source of the object and then rebuilds on the other resource -- instead of serializing to byte code.  However, `ppft` uses `dill.source.importable`, which for modules either gets the full source or an short string with an import (e.g. "`import readline\n`").  You can see this behavior below:

```
>>> import dill
>>> import readline
>>> 
>>> dill.dumps(readline)
'\x80\x02cdill.dill\n_import_module\nq\x00U\x08readlineq\x01\x85q\x02Rq\x03.'
>>> dill.copy(readline)
<module 'readline' from '/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/readline/readline.so'>
>>> dill.check(readline)
<module 'readline' from '/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/readline/readline.so'>
>>> dill.source.importable(readline)
'import readline\n'
>>>
```
So, in the above, `copy` is a `dumps` and `loads` where object sharing is allowed (like done in `multiprocess` on linux), and `check` is a `copy` where object sharing is not allowed (as done on windows or in `ppft`).  You can see it handles `readline` just fine.

So, here's a function with `readline` in it: 
```
>>> def doit(x):
...   import readline
...   return x
… 
>>>
>>> dill.dumps(doit)
'\x80\x02cdill.dill\n_create_function\nq\x00(cdill.dill\n_load_type\nq\x01U\x08CodeTypeq\x02\x85q\x03Rq\x04(K\x01K\x02K\x02KCU\x10d\x01\x00d\x00\x00l\x00\x00}\x01\x00|\x00\x00Sq\x05NJ\xff\xff\xff\xff\x86q\x06U\x08readlineq\x07\x85q\x08U\x01xq\th\x07\x86q\nU\x07<stdin>q\x0bU\x04doitq\x0cK\x01U\x04\x00\x01\x0c\x01q\r))tq\x0eRq\x0fc__builtin__\n__main__\nh\x0cNN}q\x10tq\x11Rq\x12.'
>>> dill.copy(doit)
<function doit at 0x107c3ba28>
>>> dill.check(doit)
<function doit at 0x10c2da2a8>
>>> dill.source.importable(doit)
'def doit(x):\n  import readline\n  return x\n'
>>> 
```
Again, this works.

So now, in `ppft` (via `pathos`)…  you can see there's some issues…
```
>>> import pathos
>>> pool = pathos.pools.ParallelPool(2)
>>> pool
<pool ParallelPool(ncpus=2, servers=None)>
>>> res = pool.imap(doit, range(2))

Python(691,0x108a2c000) malloc: *** mmap(size=1971238742399336448) failed (error code=12)

```

Is this something like what you are seeing?  (it also hangs for `map` instead of `imap` above)

If so, I might understand what's causing it, but am not sure how to fix it just yet.  `map` has to allocate the correct-sized memory for the object that is being passed, and if it were to poorly estimate the size of the object, it might throw a `malloc_error`… and I'm wondering if that's happening due to the use of "`import readline\n`" as opposed to the actual size of the module object itself in python.  I'm not sure… but it seems to be a bug.

Any more information you can provide would be helpful… especially if you are seeing a different traceback than the above.

You're getting a couple test failures because of tests that expected output from codegen to be formatted a particular way, which is now different.

See:
`tests/python/pants_test/tasks/test_protobuf_integration.py`, the `test_source_ordering()` test-case.
The culprit is this line, which looks for generated .proto files:
`match = re.search(r'(?P<sequence>\d+)\.proto\\?$', line)`
It's choking because the output has extra dots in it, so the line is ending in a '.', which that regex doesn't permit. Easiest solution:
`match = re.search(r'(?P<sequence>\d+)\.proto[\\.]?$', line)`

It looks like `tests/python/pants_test/python/test_python_run_integration.py` is also having trouble, in `_maybe_run_version`.

Interesting link: 
[openxml standard](https://www.ecma-international.org/news/TC45_current_work/Office%20Open%20XML%20Part%204%20-%20Markup%20Language%20Reference.pdf) page 1032 - Bookmarks
Quote of above document about the bookmarks:
## Bookmarks
Within a WordprocessingML document, bookmarks refer to arbitrary regions of content which are bounded and
have a unique name associated with them.
Because bookmarks are a legacy word processing function which predates the concepts of XML and wellformedness,
they can start and end at any location within a document's contents and therefore must use the
"cross-structure" annotation format described in §2.13.2.
[Example: Consider the following WordprocessingML markup for two paragraphs, each reading Example Text, where a bookmark has been added spanning the second word in paragraph one and the first word in paragraph two. The bookmarkStart and bookmarkEnd elements (§2.13.6.2; §2.13.6.1) specify the location where the bookmark starts and ends, but cannot contain it using a single tag because it spans part of two paragraphs. 
However, the two tags are part of one group because the id attribute value specifies 0 for both. end example]
The example:
## XML:
```XML
<w:p>
   <w:r>
     <w:t>Example</w:t>
   </w:r>
   <w:bookmarkStart w:id="0" w:name="sampleBookmark" />
   <w:r>
     <w:t xml:space="preserve"> text.</w:t>
   </w:r>
</w:p>
<w:p>
  <w:r>
    <w:t>Example</w:t>
  </w:r>
  <w:bookmarkEnd w:id="0" />
  <w:r>
    <w:t xml:space="preserve"> text.</w:t>
  </w:r>
</w:p>
```

### bookmarkStart
This element specifies the start of a bookmark within a WordprocessingML document. This start marker is
matched with the appropriately paired end marker by matching the value of the id attribute from the associated
bookmarkEnd element.
If no bookmarkEnd element exists subsequent to this element in document order with a matching id attribute
value, then this element is ignored and no bookmark is present in the document with this name.
If a bookmark begins and ends within a single table, it is possible for that bookmark to cover discontiguous parts
of that table which are logically related (e.g. a single column in a table). This type of placement for a bookmark is
accomplished (and described in detail) on the colFirst and colLast attributes on this element.
[Example: Consider a document with a bookmark which spans half of paragraph one, and part of paragraph two. The bookmarkStart element specifies the start of the region for the testing123 bookmark. This element is then linked to the bookmarkEnd element which also has an id attribute value of 0. end example]

### bookmarkEnd
This element specifies the end of a bookmark within a WordprocessingML document. This end marker is
matched with the appropriately paired start marker by matching the value of the id attribute from the
associated bookmarkStart element.
If no bookmarkStart element exists prior to this element in document order with a matching id attribute value,
then this element is ignored and no bookmark is present in the document with this name.
[Example: Consider a document with a bookmark which spans half of paragraph one, and part of paragraph two. The bookmarkEnd element specifies the end of the region for the bookmark whose bookmarkStart element
has an id attribute value of 0. In this case, this refers to the testing123 bookmark. end example]

The following WordprocessingML illustrates an example of content which fufills this constraint:
```XML
<w:p>
   <w:r>
     <w:t xml:space="preserve">This is sentence one.</w:t>
   </w:r>
   <w:bookmarkStart w:id="0" w:name="testing123"/>
   <w:r>
     <w:t>This is sentence two.</w:t>
   </w:r>
</w:p>
<w:p>
   <w:r>
     <w:t xml:space="preserve">This </w:t>
   </w:r>
   <w:bookmarkEnd w:id="0"/>
   <w:r>
     <w:t>is sentence three.</w:t>
   </w:r>
</w:p>

Explanation from README:
`IMPORTANT: nodes representing distinct pieces of content MUST have distinct source_ids. Each node has a content_id (computed as a function of the source_domain and the node's source_id) that uniquely identifies a piece of content within Kolibri for tracking purposes. For example, if the same video occurs in multiple places in the tree, you would use the same source_id for those nodes -- but content nodes that aren't for that video need to have different source_ids.`

That is understandable, because you might be serving resources at http://0.0.0.0:5000/ which is not the way to go. The interface `0.0.0.0` is not a valid interface from the client's perspective, it is internal to the service. The service should not tell the world that it is listening to the traffic at `0.0.0.0`, instead it can tell it is listening on `localhost` or any other hostname or IP address as configured.

Ps. Note that in the fontmake workflow there is one more caveat: sometimes, in one font, a glyph cam be an outline glyph (made out of contours) and in some other font it can be a composite glyph (made from references to other glyphs and not containing contours on its own). This is also considered incompatible. 

All the compatibility issues could be resolved automatically: 
1. If at least one font has the glyph as an outline glyph, the fonts which have the corresponding glyph as a composite glyph should "decompose" the glyph. 
2. If at least one font has a segment which is a curve, the corresponding segments in all other fonts for that glyph should be converted from line to curve. 
3. If the length of the glyphs differ i.e. the glyphs have different numbers of points, "compatibilization" should be performed. This can be done algorithmically to a large extent, and the concepts are explained in this video: https://vimeo.com/123781569 (fast forward to 21:50).

That is an interesting question! The approach you're talking about is something I played with a long time ago in my dissertation and it can actually be done natively in SYMPHONY (at least in principle---as far as i know, the option hasn't been used in more than decade). 

In DipPy, I think you can make it work in basically the way you're thinking, although there would be some overhead and I'm not sure if you would gain anything. In Dantzig-Wolfe, the solutions to the subproblem have to be in the space of solutions to the original problem and the columns of the master problem correspond to solutions to that subproblem. Solutions to the subproblem are combined to produce potential solutions to the original problem.

The space of variables in your original problem is the edges, so to make it work, you would have to return unit vectors as the solutions to your "subproblem" (i.e., your subproblem solutions would be a graph with a single edge). The solution to the original problem would then end up being a combination of these unit vectors, i.e., a combination of edges from the original graph.  The columns in your master would just be the columns from the original constraint matrix since you would just be multiplying the original constraint matrix by a unit vector.

Technically, since your subproblem would have no constraints (other than variable bounds and integrality), you could really return anything you want, but if you return non-trivial solutions, then you wouldn't be implementing  the method you're after. All in all, I don't see any reason this wouldn't work. As you say, it's really nothing more than having a box-constrained subproblem. Definitely do let me know how it goes if you try it.

I simply deployed a new cluster and modified [deis-controller](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/deisctl/units/deis-controller.service) and [deis-logger](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/deisctl/units/deis-logger.service) to no longer depend on `deis-store-volume`.

I configured all settings beforehand, similar to what you did. Yes, we need to specify S3 settings twice, both for gateway and for registry. The reason for that is because of the `create_bucket` conf.d. script in `deis-registry` ( https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/registry/templates/create_bucket ). Without store-gateway settings, confd will just wait forever.

I then deployed the unit files I needed manually via `deisctl install`, which was: `SERVICES=("logspout" "logger" "registry" "controller" "builder" "publisher" "router")`

This made `controller` and `logger` crash since `/var/lib/deis/store` was missing, so I created that folder manually (which will cause issues if a server goes down...so I need to automate it, but that shouldn't be a big issue).

Biggest change between your way and my way, is that in my way I only install the platform unit files which I need, avoiding to install the database and all the store stuff.

I think a lot could be solved by a `deis-store-volume` compability unit file. The file should do something like this:

We do verify that the file is present on the filesystem before launching the networking process.  I suspect we need to try one of the following.

1. Modify the check to actually try to establish a socket connection.
2. Move the check to within the networking process startup.

And for either, we should add handling for this error so that we can shutdown cleanly and output a more user friendly error message.

>  rlp/codec.py:72(encode_raw)

This one is tougher, but here is one way to reduce the calls of `isinstance` by 2/3rds:

_old code_

```py
    if isinstance(item, Atomic):
        if len(item) == 1 and item[0] < 128:
            return item
        payload = item
        prefix_offset = 128  # string
    elif not isinstance(item, str) and isinstance(item, collections.Sequence):
        payload = b''.join(encode_raw(x) for x in item)
        prefix_offset = 192  # list
    else:
        msg = 'Cannot encode object of type {0}'.format(type(item).__name__)
        raise EncodingError(msg, item)
```

_new code_

```py
    if isinstance(item, Atomic):
        if len(item) == 1 and item[0] < 128:
            return item
        payload = item
        prefix_offset = 128  # string
    else:
        try:
            assert not isinstance(item, str)
        except AssertionError as exc:
            raise encoding_error(item) from exc

        try:
            payload = b''.join(encode_raw(x) for x in item)
        except TypeError as exc:
            raise encoding_error(item) from exc

        prefix_offset = 192  # list

def encoding_error(item)
        msg = 'Cannot encode object of type {0}'.format(type(item).__name__)
        return EncodingError(msg, item)
```

`eth-keys` is a low level tool for doing the ECDSA operations.  it uses canonical `v` values of `0` and `1`, where-as the EVM uses `27` and `28`.

Additionally, I'm not 100% sure that the implementation produces deterministic signatures.

Additionally, `web3.eth.sign` prefixes the message being signed prior to signing it.

So....

1. They aren't expected to be the same due to the differences in `v` values
2. They *may* not be the same even if you make the `v` values the same.
3. They won't be the same due to the appended prefix used in `web3.eth.sign`.
Practical proposals still welcome.

The issue still stands that older faulty build need to be ignored (the `conda` issue) or pulled from the repository (which makes it a `bioconda` issue).
Re: the `conda` issue: Currently there is no functionality on the repository side to mark builds as defunct or superseded which only leaves the resolution given by @mcg1969: those builds need to be removed from the repository. For more details on that, see #4956 and @mcg1969's Gist at https://gist.github.com/mcg1969/38589eeefb046c417720f1027f97085b.

To still reproduce the issue, see:
```bash
$ conda create -n env -c bioconda --dry-run htslib 'pysam<0.11'
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /home/maba/miniconda3/envs/env:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    bcftools:   1.4.1-0       bioconda
    bzip2:      1.0.6-3               
    curl:       7.52.1-0              
    htslib:     1.4.1-0       bioconda
    libgcc:     5.2.0-0
