```bash
    pysam:      0.10.0-py35_2 bioconda
    python:     3.5.3-1               
    readline:   6.2-2                 
    samtools:   1.4.1-0       bioconda
    setuptools: 27.2.0-py35_0         
    sqlite:     3.13.0-0              
    tk:         8.5.18-0              
    wheel:      0.29.0-py35_0         
    xz:         5.2.2-1               
    zlib:       1.2.8-3               
```
If `htslib` is one of the explicitly requested packages, `conda` tries to maximize its version and thus picks up `htslib-1.4.1-0` but also `pysam-0.10.0-py35_2`. This `pysam` build is faulty in that it should require `htslib >=1.3,<1.4` but only requires `htslib >=1.3`. Outcome: The above command will install a non-functional `pysam` package.
```bash
$ conda create -n env -c bioconda --dry-run 'pysam<0.11'
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /home/maba/miniconda3/envs/env:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    bcftools:   1.3.1-1       bioconda

    xz:         5.2.2-1

```
It `htslib` is not explicitly requested, only `pysam`'s version (including its build number) gets maximized so that `pysam-0.10.0-py36_3` can be used.

> How comes it has exactly 0 downloads?

It is new, and I have probably been the only one using it.  I probably installed it prior to it being made public.  Thus, 0 downloads (nothing depends on it, and my one install to get things working came from our internal test server).

> Is it really needed?

It saves me from having to install a development environment.  My setup consists of installing miniconda, then installing a metapackage that brings in (this) scp, and many other utilities for packaging on windows (gnuwin32, git_windows, Putty's plink as ssh - another one I will probably have to rename) In that, it is very convenient.  Not strictly necessary, but I think it builds nicely towards conda as a self-contained CI bootstrapping tool.

> This is definitely going to conflict with scp (which has been on Conda for nearly two years at pypi/scp, btw...

This was my bad.  I did not do my homework.  There was no scp in our anaconda recipes, and I did not look at conda-recipes.

> is the pypi repository is even official?

I'll have to get back to you on that.  I don't know.

> If this is an internal tool, couldn't you name it something unique, like scp-exe-internal-conda-build, or better yet, not add it to the registry at all?

This need not be an internal tool.  I made a package because I thought other people might find it useful.  I don't know how you came across it.  I would like to.  I'm very sorry that you found it sinister.

> with its broken patch package

This is fixed: https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/424 was available as patch-2.5.0.  @groutr cleaned up the recipe later (patch-2.5.9-0), but it was broken by not including the manifest, accidentally (this was the issue where patch required elevation).  Patch-2.5.9-1 has the manifest, and should work.  It has been available on win-64 for 2 weeks or so.  Sorry it took us time to fix this.  Our internal build systems have been Python 2, and now that other people are building with Python 3 core environments, we're having to go back and address some issues like this.

> the random failures unlinking the _test environment

Please link me to an issue, I'd like to investigate and make sure that we are following up on this.

> the non-functional envs

This was fixed by your PR (thanks).  Sorry it took so long for us to merge.  Conda and our whole system on Windows has not had the attention it deserves for some time.  We are working hard to change that (I'm proud of #1727), and I hope you'll see the fruits of our efforts in new releases soon.  Many of these unfortunately will not be ready in time for the coming Anaconda 2.4 release.

> does it really need to be there?

This is a subjective question.  If it makes it easier to set up CI on AppVeyor, or for anyone else to set up a Windows build build environment for even one person other than me, then I think it is a good thing.

I'm sorry you've had issues with Conda lately.  We are working hard to improve it, and we especially appreciate users like you who have contributed to improving the tools. > I am/was contemplating appending to the user's variable PATH. That is still something set outside of the cmd.exe session but is not a system variable change

What I meant here is that for that user, such a change would be system-wide (to my mind, if it's stored in the Windows Registry, then it is a system issue) and it *will* break other software. IMHO this is not something we can even begin to contemplate. On Linux and OS X, software uses RPATHs to make PATH changes less of an issue* but Windows does not have any equivalent concept unfortunately.

> the best I can do that is somewhat safe is to derive the path of sourced files from the process' ID (if an absolute/relative path is given use that, otherwise assume conda is on the PATH).

bash, dash, posh, zsh, ksh, csh, tcsh: In general, on any Unix shell you have such fallbacks as those listed here: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2563300/how-can-i-find-the-location-of-the-tcsh-shell-script-im-executing
For a batch file: %~dp0
Cygwin, MSYS2: Depends on the Unix shell, is no different from the same shell on Unix modulus perhaps calling `$(cygpath -wm)` or `$(cygpath -w)`.
PowerShell: I don't know, I never use this.

> If you have enlightening ways to detect the path to sourced files in a cross platform and cross shell manner I would love to learn.

OK, another alternative I just cooked up that works on both macOS and Linux:

```
#!/usr/bin/env bash

ME=$(lsof -p $$ | grep activate.sh | grep -o '[^ ]*$')
echo ME is $ME
```

> Otherwise we would need to introduce a new environment variable (CONDA_ROOT) to tell the scripts where to find stuff

We already have this, it is called `${CONDA_PREFIX}`

*Even on Linux or macOS, I would not recommend a user of Anaconda/Minconda to add it to their PATH in a startup script because you are then at the mercy of how well behaved other software is.
http://bugs.python.org/issue9253

> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io?src=pr). Last update [aed3f9c...a7c2222](https://codecov.io/gh/conda/conda/compare/aed3f9cf6d9daa66d920f2ad5dc435b5588acc41...a7c2222d5f1d9a08315014813b786c8149376574?src=pr)

That's right, Continuum maintains the copyright in the license. This is how open source licenses generally work. I have neither the knowledge nor the time to really explain this well, since I am not a lawyer. Hence what I say below may be wrong, and SHOULD NOT BE TAKEN AS LEGAL ADVICE. 

My basic understanding is that, under US law, copyright is automatically assigned to any work as soon as it is created. That work may then be licensed for others to use by the copyright holder by whatever terms the holder wants. The BSD open source license is one such set of terms. It allows almost completely unrestricted use of the work, basically subject to the terms listed in the license (such as the requirement to maintain the copyright and disclaimer text in derivative works). 

It is also possible to completely remove the copyright of a work, which puts it in the public domain, but this is generally frowned upon for a few legal reasons (e.g., it is not clear if US law even allows works to be irrevocably placed in the public domain, and the concept of public domain does not even exist in some countries). It's much smarter to license the work under a license like the BSD license. This protects the copyright holder (in this case, Continuum Analytics) with the disclaimer. This has the intended effect of putting the thing in the public domain, namely, other people can reuse the source code basically however they want, but it also limits the copyright holder's liability with the disclaimer, and the copyright notice rules maintain attribution.  But you can see for instance that conda includes versioneer, which has been put in the public domain.

You can spend a lot of time learning about this stuff, and sometimes there are no straight answers because things have to be tested in courts, but at the end of the day, practically, you should just license your code with a [common open source license](http://choosealicense.com/), as they have been written by lawyers who have thought a lot more about these things than you have. 

As an aside, Miniconda itself, as well as Anaconda (the installers) have a separate license, which is displayed when you run them.

I don't think this can be fixed then, as you are currently using conda. If conda can't write to the conda-bld directory in the root environment directory, it uses `~/conda-bld`. But `~` is going to be a different directory when you are sudo, so even if we made `--use-local` fallback, it would look in a different place than where the package is. 

The easiest fix for you is to add

```
conda-build: /Users/hugo/conda-bld
```

to your `.condarc` (to whatever rc file will be seen by sudo).

I wish I understood why installing from wheel works as well.

The root cause involves how the relative shared library paths are set.
 pysam need relative shared library paths because the pysam code is used as
a module, but also as a library to link against by packages that build on
pysam.  Native Python doesn’t really address this use-case and it took some
fairly hairy surgery to the build system to get it to work at all.  The
magic is performed by the cy_build.py module, which sets linker flags and
tries to account for the slightly different install paths and processes
used by setuptools and pip.

Hope that helps...

Part of the reason you may be having read issues with your indices is that they're hourly.  Elasticsearch automatically "times out" an index after 30 minutes, reclaiming the resources allocated to "active" indices.  This would leave a decent chunk of memory unavailable for 30 minutes of the next index's hour.  That said, how do you know you've reduced overhead by setting indices to read-only? 

This feature has been requested before, so this is actually a duplicate of #160 

It is, maybe, perhaps, finally time to add the feature.   I am reluctant to do so because there will be those who set recent indices to read-only and blow-up their Logstash instances because old data sometimes straggles in from the past.  This will cause errors and all kinds of _fun_ as Elasticsearch pushes back and says it can't write to that index.

Thanks @HonzaKral. FWIW, the reason we are using the (deprecated) URL userinfo is we are migrating our codebase from base ES to ES+Shield and the ability to inject credentials without touching ES client constructors saves a lot of time.

Bulk indexing can be an expensive process so it can indeed take a long time. If it takes more than 10 seconds the only soution is to raise the `timeout` parameter of the es client. Either for the whole instance or you can specify `request_timeout=30` as part of the `es.bulk` call. There is no way to speed it up from the client - either send smaller bulk requests or raise the timeout value.

also note that you are replicating the chunking logic that we already have as part of the bulk helper: http://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.org/en/latest/helpers.html#elasticsearch.helpers.bulk

Hope this helps...

Issue:

Doing `az keyvault certificate get-default-policy > defaultpolicy.json` would generate a file UTF-16LE encoded under PowerShell.

Although, azure-cli is supposed to handle it... my bet is that it fails to detect UTF-16LE and uses UTF-16 instead which transforms it to a deserialization error instead of an encoding error.

Workaround for those walking this path in the future:

```powershell
az keyvault certificate get-default-policy | Out-File -Encoding utf8 defaultpolicy.json
```

This forces the encoding to UTF-8 and the `az` command can now be used properly in PowerShell like so by escaping the `@`.

```powershell
az keyvault certificate create --vault-name my-keyvault -n cert --policy `@defaultpolicy.json
```

@jbcrail and @martindurant if the user requests a delimited block, and there are no delimiters at all within a 4mb chunk you **must** throw. Appending the data with a warning is dangerous here, as the user may not ever see the warning and, down the road in the system, their data will be corrupt when running jobs, which will result in failures much later in the pipeline. That in turn will result in an escalation to our service engineers to investigate why their data doesn't have the boundaries they thought it had.
In this case, if the call has bad data that they want delimited, it is best to fail immediately, and force them to make a decision:
1. fix their data to have valid delimiters where no record is > 4MB
2. Change their delimiter to satisfy the record limit
3. Upload their data without delimiters, which forces the user to explicitly acknowledge that their data will not be delimited on the server.

To clarify a little bit my point of view:

- while I totally agree that sometime domain-specific languages are the answer, they come with [a huge cost](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain-specific_language#Advantages_and_disadvantages]. In the case of `pre-commit` configuration, IMHO simplicity is more important than keeping the verbosity low (aka "better stupid JSON than a dedicated custom perfectly-fitting language" aka KISS & YAGNI)

- I'm only discussing the choice of a new configuration language here, to replace YAML. Half of the issues you're mentioning in this issue (top-level structure & namespacing) could be solved by reorganizing the config/manifest structure without switching to another language.

- about `ConfigParser`, there are various solutions to handle lists: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/335695/lists-in-configparser
But round tripping has to be added for sure.

- `commentjson` has [a fairly simple implementation](https://github.com/vaidik/commentjson/blob/master/commentjson/commentjson.py). It looks to me more interesting to contribute to this project or take inspiration from it in order to build a lib than handle round trippable JSON with comments, instead of creating [yet-another-language](https://xkcd.com/927).

Finally, is there a place to discuss `dumbconf` spec ?

@erwincoumans Godot has a tree-like node structure, so the global transform is in general a product of multiple local transformations in general. While each local basis is just a rotation and scale, Bi = Ri.Si, a composite basis can have things like shear (example: a child node rotated around z-axis by 45 degrees and a parent node with scaling 2,1,1 results in a general affine matrix which can't be decomposed into a global rotation and a scaling).

OK, I (and others) have written some nbextensions ans server extensions and package them with conda. Because we want to keep thing isolated inside prefix, we want to read the config from `prefix/etc/jupyter/nbconfig` but some people have also some other extensions installed in the user space (aka. `./jupyter`).
If we setup the `jupyter_config_dir` to just look into `prefix/etc/jupyter` all the "user" activated extensions are not loaded... this is the main discussion in the other issues.

To workaround this for the moment I have written a custom ConfigManager which is able to read the "user" space but also the prefix space, so people can install our extensions and also install other extensions as well and have all of them activated beyond the place where the the config is located.

In fact, I have no problem to avoid `config=True` at the native ConfigManager, we can remove that if you feel it will bring problems. The main thing is avoid hardcoding the `config_dir` at the notebookapp level so anyone can come up with another ConfigManager implementation to support their use case (like the one I described above) or any other use case that the the native implementation does not support...

Should we add the check to avoid multi-style xml-field definition to force use `<field name=***` instead of `<field ** name=*`

Different style (eval first):
```xml
<record model="ir.rule" id="access_rule">
    <field name="name">Access rule</field>
    <field name="model_id" ref="model_test_model"/>
    <field eval="1" name="perm_read"/>
    <field eval="0" name="perm_create"/>
    <field eval="0" name="perm_write"/>
    <field eval="0" name="perm_unlink"/>
    <field name="domain_force">[('user_ids','in',user.id)]</field>
</record>
```

Unique style
```xml
<record model="ir.rule" id="access_rule">
    <field name="name">Access rule</field>
    <field name="model_id" ref="model_test_model"/>
    <field name="perm_read" eval="1"/>
    <field name="perm_create" eval="0"/>
    <field name="perm_write" eval="0"/>
    <field name="perm_unlink" eval="0"/>
    <field name="domain_force">[('user_ids','in',user.id)]</field>
</record>
```

This allow us use a same standard style, and avoid large diff for changes of custom standards.
This check don't affect the process.

What do you think?

> On the other hand, is there value in making Schematics behave more like other systems with the concept of schemas (and default and required-ness), particularly from a learner perspective?

Well, we already have experience with the current system, and in purely practical terms, the `None`/unset issue has been raised so many times that it's got to be addressed one way or another.

In general, the existence of a schema does not mean you *must* have a value, even a null, for every field. Relational databases do enforce this, but Schematics is a data processing library, not a persistence mechanism. It's quite normal for even schema-conforming data transmission formats to omit nonexistent values.

Is the controversy here about the entire concept of maintaining records of omitted values? Or is it mostly about how the absence of a value is exposed by the API (simulating a missing attribute)? Because that is not set in stone.

In [this comment](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/issues/263#issuecomment-150047136) I requested feedback on two alternatives I was considering. The other approach was also based on keeping tabs on `Undefined` objects in the internal `_data` structure. However, these would normally be exposed as `None`s. The "undefined" status of a field would only come into play if specifically requested:

```python
class M(Model):
    a, b, c = IntType(), IntType(), IntType()
```

```python
>>> m = M({'a': 1, 'b': None})
>>> m.a
1
>>> m.b is None
True
>>> isset(m.b)
True
>>> m.c is None
True
>>> isset(m.c)
False
>>> m.to_primitive()
{'a': 1, 'b': None, 'c': None}
>>> m.to_primitive(serialize_when_undefined=False)
{'a': 1, 'b': None}
```

This touches on something I thought about a few days ago... What if `to_primitive()` and `to_native()` were defined only in `BaseType` and `MultiType`, and they would defer to the appropriate private method such as `_to_primitive()` on the intended field?

The original motivation was to make importing and exporting extensible. It would be easy to hook up new functionality to fields if the method calls always went through the base class.

But this could also be exploited to hide the `export_loop`/`to_primitive` dichotomy:

* Calling `StringType.to_primitive()` would hit `BaseType.to_primitive()`, which would then call the actual target `StringType._to_primitive()`.

* Calling `ListType.to_primitive()` would invoke `MultiType.to_primitive()`, which would call `ListType.export_loop()`.

You could try installing from a git clone of the source instead of from pip. That is, something like

```
git clone https://github.com/hips/autograd.git
pip install -e autograd
```

Here are two ways to do it. Because `func` is a linear map, you can implement it in a sparse matrix form treating `a` and `b` as coefficients on fixed basis elements:

```python
def func(a, b):
    def one_hot(i, j):
        out = np.zeros((10, 10))
        out[i, j] = 1
        return out
    return a * one_hot(5, 7) + b * one_hot(3, 6)
```

If the indices (5, 7) and (3, 6) are really constants (or you're going to call `func` many times with the same values for those indices) you can make that more efficient with staged evaluation, so that the basis elements aren't re-allocated on every invocation.

Another way to do it is to write your own primitive, which is probably both more straightforward and more performant:

```python
@primitive
def func(a, b):
    m = np.zeros((10, 10))
    m[5, 7] = a
    m[3, 6] = b
    return m
func3.defvjp(lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, a, b: g[5, 7], argnum=0)
func3.defvjp(lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, a, b: g[3, 6], argnum=1)
```

Inside a primitive, there are no autograd boxed values; everything is raw numpy, so you're back in the world you're used to. You have to define any vjp's you need for your primitives, though.

When writing custom primitives, `check_grads` is your friend!

Side notes: of course, you only need to do this if you're going to differentiate through `func`. Assignment into autograd's ArrayNodes is not supported, but when you're using `autograd.numpy`, ArrayNodes are only created as necessary during tracing, i.e. when an `autograd.numpy` function gets an ArrayNode as one of its arguments; otherwise, it's just raw numpy as usual. In fact, the kind of assignment you wrote might work anyway, since `m` is being created as a zeros matrix and won't be an ArrayNode, though when `a` or `b` is an ArrayNode, the assignment would have to turn it into  an array with dtype object and things might get weird.

Running the 'other' phase after the `@action` handler is the most compelling part of the design to me. If the 'other' phase isn't run, then there is little reason to couple actions with the reactive framework at all. I cannot share handlers between hooks and actions, and instead need to code my action in a linear sequence of operations the way we used to code hooks. The `@setup` handlers would be run, preparing the environment for a main event loop that will never be run. The action defined in my layer cannot trigger handlers defined in the charms using my layer.

Its worth mentioning that writing actions in this fashion is opt-in. The charmer needs to copy the template to the actions/action-name path (manually, or in the future by declaring it in layer.yaml and having charm build do it). If people want a self contained action that does not trigger reactive states they can continue to do so like they do today. They could even import charms.reactive and alter state of future hooks (we could provide helpers for that). What they can't do is, for example, change the service's administrative password and alter the reactive state causing the existing handlers to kick in and publish the updated password everywhere. And they especially can't do that if the action is defined in a layer and the handlers that need to be invoked defined in a charm using that layer.

The state portion in https://github.com/juju-solutions/layer-basic/pull/69 can certainly be done away with, as to my mind it just provides an alternate spelling (and could be implemented in a layer with a `@setup` handler if people really preferred that spelling).

There is a problem with manually installing toil-scripts on the leader in a virtualenv. If done naively, a duplicate, potentially conflicting version of Toil would be installed in that virtualenv, while what you really want is for the virtualenv to inherit the globally installed version of Toil that ToilBox ships with. Someone with in-depth knowledge of Python packaging should actually try this out.

I almost feel that toil-scripts' setup.py should not explicitly declare a `install_requires` dependency on toil. Rather, it should explicitly check for it's existence and version before calling `setup()`.

`atomic` wouldn't solve the potential race condition in your example, as the instance has already been loaded from the db, so the value of `self.amount_allocated` can't be trusted any more.

even if the instance was loaded from the db _within_ the atomic transaction I don't think you can trust it as the transaction does not preclude another user session from changing the value behind your back (assuming default isolation level https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/databases/#isolation-level http://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/transaction-iso.html#XACT-READ-COMMITTED) 

this is why `select_for_update` exists - i.e. in the case where you `get()` the row then later `save()` it, the db would ensure that row is locked

however _I think_ I have gone too far in my example and `select_for_update` is not necessary when doing an operation like `.filter(pk=self.pk).update(amount_allocated=F('amount_allocated') + amount)` since this accomplishes the update within a single query, so it's enough to just be within an atomic transaction

> Its completely within the realm and resources of Microsoft to make a cross-platform desktop UI system.

Yes but there is very little business case for this. You are talking about desktop-only development (a niche already), where Mac usage is critical enough to warrant an abstraction layer (a much smaller niche: hobbyist apps, design apps?), but which don't need tailored design (intersection is approximately zero).

>GitHub made one for crying out loud, called Electron.

This is not a desktop platform. It is both desktop and mobile. It's also terrible.

> the reason why they're still dinking around with Windows only UI platforms

Any serious OS has a UI platform. Windows has one. iOS has one. Android has one. Mac has one. A cross-platform UI system has to sit on top of native UI systems.

> a good .NET based cross platform desktop UI is what developers want... [Microsoft's] upper level strategists are dinosaurs still holding onto the glory years

As everyone knows Microsoft lost out in mobile. Windows is just as dominant as ever in desktops. A cross-platform framework that is desktop-only is not solving any serious business problem.

> Xamarin ... fine, its time to evolve past it. ... You make an abstraction over OpenGL and DirectX like every game engine ever made and layer a logical UI system over it.

I assume your suggestion is to evolve Xamarin to use platform-independent graphics rendering. That's not too hard; you can implement SkiaSharp as a target platform effectively. The advantages are debatable. You would still need native controls in a lot of places (e.g. phone calls, media playback). And you can implement this piecemeal, one control at a time, as an option for developers. So really this is a much less dramatic suggestion than it sounds. And some of the work is ongoing in Xamarin.Forms. Slowly because demand is not very great for this approach.

> When an OS version reaches end-of-life as defined by the OS owner, .NET Core will also cease to provide support for that OS version. Previously released packages will remain available for customers needing access but formal support and updates of any kind will no long be provided.

Right now Let's Encrypt would never issue for `www.exāmple.org` because we don't issue for any IDN names.

https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/issues/597

We have had a lot of discussions on our forums about the general issue of whether users may think that the padlock means that the site is non-malicious or has been vetted in some way. We hope HTTPS will come to be used for all web sites all the time, so we don't think it can or should ultimately be regarded as a signal that a site is not malicious.

If there's something we can do to try to improve users' understanding of this issue, I think that would be great, but I'm not sure the "How it Works" page is a helpful place to try to go into this (I think that page is overwhelmingly read by prospective certificate subscribers rather than prospective certificate relying parties).

@abalkin The issue is that the `_DatetimeWithFold` implementation is inconsistent between Python 3.5 and 3.6 because it doesn't implement PEP495 equality, so on Python 3.5:

```python
>>> from datetime import datetime, timezone
>>> from dateutil import tz
>>> d1 = datetime(2016, 11, 6, 6, 12, 15, 0, timezone.utc)
>>> d1 == d1.astimezone(tz.gettz('US/Eastern'))
True
```

But in Python 3.6, as demonstrated above, that resolves to `False`.  Since you designed the original `_DatetimeWithFold` implementation, I was wondering if you would have a recommendation on whether I should backport the equality feature into the `_DatetimeWithFold` implementation or if you see another easy way to resolve this to keep the 3.5 -> 3.6 transition backwards compatible.

Hey,

redirecting the MAD-X output to an arbitrary file handle can be achieved as follows: 

```
from cpymad.madx import Madx
from cpymad import _rpc
import subprocess

def madx_redirect_stdio(stdout=subprocess.STDOUT, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT):
        with open(os.devnull, 'r') as devnull:
            client, process = _rpc.LibMadxClient.spawn_subprocess(
                stdout=stdout,
                stderr=stderr,
                stdin=devnull,
                bufsize=0)         # unbuffered IO
        madx = Madx(libmadx=client.libmadx)

# drop all stdio:
devnull = open(os.devnull, 'w')
madx_redirect_stdio(devnull, devnull)
```

See also the code in [MadGUI](https://github.com/hibtc/madgui/blob/master/madgui/component/session.py#L65).

I admit, this is a bit hacky and the documentation is almost nonexisting. Probably its a common demand and should be made easier or at least documented. I'll try to add something as soon as I get some time (currently busy with something else, sorry).

Hope, this helps.

C'mon.. None of this improvements look vital to me:
* Changed implementation from double linked list to simple list. OK, it gets O(1) index at cost of O(n) deletes. Srsly? The main three operations on sets are addition, removal and check for inclusion. All three operations are O(1) in current implementation. Iteration is O(n) which is expected, no overhead here. Now it adds indexing. Why we should care about indexing at all?
* Pickling. I tried existing one and it does support pickling as well. It might not be the most optimal one, but it does work if you will need that (although the use case is also obscure: it's a strange idea to pickle schema descriptors).
* getitem is probably from the same use case as indexing which I do not understand.

Given OrderedSet is used to hold filed names and there are no removals used, the proposed implementation will not make it worse. But I still consider it to be a worse implementation (because it degrades removal time complexity) than original implementation with no apparent value added.

I see your point about preserving generality. In this case, though, I lean towards "Doing The Right Thing ™"--as you've suggested in #434 , the behavior when deserializing a float will be unexpected for the vast majority of users.

It's worth noting that django-rest-framework and colander both implement this behavior:

- https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/fields.py#L924
- https://github.com/Pylons/colander/blob/master/colander/__init__.py#L1366

Hi

It's because `rs.reply()` is a synchronous method and can't return the reply until *everything* has finished. When it's processing the tags in the reply and sees the `<call>` tag, it runs the Python `pause` function you defined, which makes it wait a couple seconds before returning a string. Then it can finally substitute that string in place of the `<call>` tag and start returning the reply.

So you'll call `rs.reply()` and it will hang for 2 seconds before returning the final reply all at once.

You're probably best off handling the delay outside the RiveScript module, like,

```
# (rivescript)
# make up a `<pause #>` tag and use it instead of `<call>`:
- a moment,  searching for best AI response <pause 2> Ok, I have it now {weight=50}
```

```python
reply = bot.reply(user, message)
parts = re.split(r'<pause \d+?>', reply)  # split at a `<pause>` tag but include the tag:
# ["a moment,  searching for best AI response ", "<pause 2>", "Ok, I have it now"]
for part in parts:
    m = re.search(r'^<pause (\d+?)>$', part)
    if m:  # this is the `<pause>` tag
        time.sleep( int(m.group(1) )  # sleep the 2 seconds
    else:
        print(part)  # or whatever
```

The scores in LightFM define the ranking: the higher the scores is for a given item, the more likely it is to be recommended. To get the top items, you should score all items and then retrieve the ones whose scores are the highest (this holds even when the scores are negative). The `Xr` in your terminology is exactly what is output by the `predict` method.

In the binary case (clicked/not clicked), the AUC score has a nice interpretation: it expresses the probability that a randomly chosen positive item (an item the user clicked) will be ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative item (an item the user did not click). Thus, an AUC of 1.0 means that the resulting ranking is perfect: no negative item is ranked higher than any positive item

I just read the comments of the new API functions and have a minor question:
I would have expected that NaN numerals raise exceptions, as their IEEE 754 bit-representation is not uniquely determined (their sign and significand bits remain under-specified). However, I do not see why +inf and -inf are underspecified, as well? According to the floating point paper ("An automatable formal semantics for IEEE-754 floating-point arithmetic"), +inf and -inf have a significand that consists entirely of 0 bits and an exponent with only 1 bits (see Sec. V.A, p. 7).

How could one detect a NaN numeral? Shouldn't we introduce another API function for that case (i.e. something like is_nan_numeral)? Otherwise, we have to rely on, e.g. catching exceptions of get_numeral_sign_bv to detect the NaN case.

Somewhat minimal working example in pure Theano:
```python
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
from theano.sandbox.rng_mrg import MRG_RandomStreams as RandomStreams
floatX = theano.config.floatX

inputs = T.matrix()
hid_init = T.vector()
rng = RandomStreams()

# Works:
def step(inp, prev):
	# rng.binomial with constant shape
    return inp + prev * rng.binomial((10,), p=.5, dtype=floatX)
out = theano.scan(step, sequences=[inputs], outputs_info=[hid_init])[0]
theano.function([inputs, hid_init], out)
print "Success!"

# Fails:
def step(inp, prev):
	# rng.binomial with symbolic shape
    return inp + prev * rng.binomial(prev.shape, p=.5, dtype=floatX)
out = theano.scan(step, sequences=[inputs], outputs_info=[hid_init])[0]
theano.function([inputs, hid_init], out)
print "Will never be reached!"
```

> These ones are just derived by passing a random normal or uniform with mean 0 and variance 1 trough the tanh and emeprically measuring the correct constant (in fact for the uniform you can find analytical formula).

Still there are multiple solutions (= combinations of alpha and beta) that give an output of mean 0 and variance 1, and you gave them for constraining beta=1 or beta=0.5. Is there any reason why beta shouldn't be something different? LeCun arrived at his combination of alpha and beta by constraining scaled_tanh(1) = 1 and retaining the variance, it seems. Hmm, no, checking myself, LeCun's recommendation of `tanh(2./3*x)*1.7159` does not retain the variance of a normal. What does it retain, actually?
I'm still a bit unsure about which factors we should have for the default `scaled_tanh`, and I think the docstring needs to explain a bit clearer how the numbers were arrived at. I also don't agree with "This tries to achieve what Batch Normalization does, but in a more simple version and with less computation." -- I think this goes too far, the objective of batch normalization is to counter the change in mean/variance caused by the network weights moving away from their well-controlled initialization, not only to counter the static influence of the nonlinearity.
So, in short, I'm fine with adding this feature, but we should reach consensus on what parameters to use for the default `scaled_tanh`, or whether we shouldn't have a default `scaled_tanh` at all.

> I don't understand this. could you please elaborate? Is it about updating the documentation ?

No, it's about rendering the documentation. The online documentation is built at readthedocs, which doesn't have a GPU. In order to render the documentation for `lasagne.layers.dnn`, Sphinx still has to be able to import `lasagne.layers.dnn` at readthedocs, despite the missing GPU support. To do that, we had to fake GPU support in the Sphinx configuration for Lasagne: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/docs/conf.py#L319-L340
If we change the GPU support check in `lasagne.layers.dnn` (https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/dnn.py#L13-L21), which we have to do in order to support both backends, then we may also have to update the faked GPU support in the Sphinx configuration (i.e., find another hack that allows to import `lasagne.layers.dnn` without a GPU).

````
Well, what about this? 

``` yaml
# required
title : my playlist

# required 
song_urls : [
"url0",
"url1",
"url2",
"url3",
"url4",
"url5",
]

# generated on first load
meta:
  - id     : 0             # references songs url array
    title  : Song title    # doesn't have to match video title
    length : 200           # just for matching purposes

  - id     : 1
    title  : Song title 2
    length : 205
```

Required part is easy to type in, and meta data part is autogenerated. If we go that way, JSON would look similar except some brackets more. Enclosing urls in quotes is required for both JSON and YAML which can be a significant overhead for users in comparison to the m3u way. I'm not sure if those #EXTINF tags are required in m3u. Are they?

Here's the JSON format for sake of comparison.

```json
{
  "title": "my playlist", 
  "song_urls": [
    "url0", 
    "url1", 
    "url2", 
    "url3", 
    "url4", 
    "url5"
  ],
 "meta": [
    {
      "length": 200, 
      "id": 0, 
      "title": "Song title"
    }, 
    {
      "length": 205, 
      "id": 1, 
      "title": "Song title 2"
    }
  ]
}
```

After some thought, I think that supporting two formats is the best option.

1. Just raw list of urls or song titles which is meant to be created by users manually.
1. JSON with meta info as suggested, but without that song_urls array.

When comparing JSON to m3u, I still find it easier to modify than m3u.

Those faulty pixels are noise, values barely above 0 % reflectance. As @adybbroe said, there's no actual signal during night on the visible and NIR channels, only some noise. My solution has been not to make composites using reflective channels during night :-) If you want to have the same composite available 24/7, then I'd add masking based on sun zenith angle (mask areas where SZA > 92 degrees, or so) for the affected channels.

I prefer to have static stretching limits for most composites, and use these limits for `overview`: R = `[0, 1]`, G = `[0, 1]`, B = `[-280, -200]` (not sure how the values should be given to the inverted infrared channel in satpy).

Facebook API 2.0 is deprecated, so when that happens, ```GraphAPI.__init__``` will go from having a useful default_version to one that isn't even supported. That means client code will have to update each instance of ```GraphAPI()```. A way to change the default DEFAULT_VERSION means client code will only have to change in one place.

I agree that this patch does do two, only tangentially-related things. But changing the default from 2.0 to something else, while intrusive to clients, is simply a reaction to the face that FB will stop supporting 2.0 very soon.

You're the boss of course, but I'd ask you to consider these points.

I looked more closely at the pagination behavior we have and I no longer believe it's possible to get into an infinite-pagination situation. We only generate a next-page header if we have a completely full page, and each time we have a completely full page, we advance the offset in the next page. I had gotten confused because I thought we would continue until `offset + len(records) == total_records`, but that isn't the case (we continue until `len(records) < limit`).

@jkimbo here are my answers. I'm not very familiar with Graphene nor Annotation. So any corrections are welcome.

> How would ObjectTypes work?

When `Hero` inherit `ObjectType`,   `ObjectType` and it's base classes' `__init_subclass_with_meta__` methods will be called. The main purpose of those `__init_subclass_with_meta__` methods is create `Hero._meta`, who stores almost all information about `Hero`, like this one:

```
In [5]: Hero._meta.fields
Out[5]: OrderedDict([
            ('name', <graphene.types.field.Field at 0x10e6e11d0>),
            ('age', <graphene.types.field.Field at 0x10e6e1278>)
        ])
```


> Could you use both ObjectTypes and AnnotatedObjectType's in the same schema?

if `AnnotatedObjectType` is a subclass of `ObjectType`, it should be easy to let them in the same schema.


> Is there anything that can be expressed in the python type system that can't be expressed in GraphQL (or vice versa)?

The only thing that I can find is that python type can do static check by tools like [mypy](https://github.com/python/mypy) or [pyre](https://github.com/facebook/pyre-check). For example:

```Shell
➜  cat -n test_type.py
     1	from typing import List
     2
     3
     4	def process_user(user_ids: List[int]):
     5	    pass
     6
     7
     8	process_user([1, 2, 3])
     9	process_user([4, 5, "NOT_INT"])
➜  mypy .
test_type.py:9: error: List item 2 has incompatible type "str"; expected "int"
```

But I don't think it's a big deal because of two reasons:

1. [typing](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html) only support python 3.5+
2. Graphene's type checker is made for request and response. There are not very "static".

> I think keeping the ObjectType and AnnotatedObjectType separate is essential, not just for keeping python 2.7 compatibility, but also supporting both ways of writing ObjectTypes.

Agree! I think a downward compatibility API is very important. We all know the story about python 2.7 😂.

Hi, I had the issue and I was able to fix it with [this commit](https://github.com/hueypeard/graphene/commit/66ed1e1410acb72bd77974710536143199a188b4).

It seems that [the following lines](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/graphql/type/typemap.py#L59-L62) in graphql-core don't correctly compare GrapheneGraphQLType types, due to no fault of graphql-core itself. To fix this and any other issues with GrapheneGraphQLTypes being equivalent, I simply added an `__eq__` comparison method to GrapheneGraphQLType to only compare the internal graphene_type and not the instances themselves.
```
if type.name in map:
  assert map[type.name] == type, (
    'Schema must contain unique named types but contains multiple types named "{}".'
  ).format(type.name)
````

I'd be happy to submit a pull request if this is, in fact, the correct way to go about solving this issue, but I can't say for sure due to my lack of experience with this library, so if any experienced graphene users can weigh in here please let me know.

My personal 2cts: graphene is pythonic, keeps your schema in sync with models, provides resolvers and filtering out of the box. graphql-core does none of that.

As you probably already know, graphene is a framework to make building graphql-core schemas easier. You can of course build your own schema by hand instead of using graphene-django. This means a lot more work depending on how many models you have. And when those models change, you'll need to make sure to also change the graphql schema, and the resolvers, etc.

With graphene, and in your case, graphene-django. Your graphql schema is generated for you from your django models. 

From the graphene-django docs, here is a simple Django model:

```
from django.db import models

class UserModel(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    last_name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
```
To create a GraphQL schema for it you simply have to write the following easy to read Python class:

```
from graphene_django import DjangoObjectType
import graphene

class User(DjangoObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = UserModel
```
Now you already have quite a lot, you have the documented model in graphiql, you get a default resolver, you can add filters quite easily, etc.

Now you could instead do this directly with graphql-core:
```
userType = GraphQLObjectType(
    'User',
    description='A User',
    fields=lambda: {
        'id': GraphQLField(
            GraphQLNonNull(GraphQLString),
            description='The id of the user.',
        ),
        'name': GraphQLField(
            GraphQLString,
            description='The name of the user.',
        ),
        'last_name': GraphQLField(
            GraphQLList(characterInterface),
            description='The last name of the user.',
            resolver=lambda user, info, **args: getUser(user),
        )
    }
)
```

It's a matter of opinion, but to my Python eyes, that is quite an eyesore, and will be hard to keep in sync with the model classes as your project grows.

And as you said, you'll then need to implement resolvers, and filtering, etc. All code you get for free with graphene-django

To clarify, the reasoning to remove `span.start_child` is because it's both superfluous and porcelain api.

Creating superfluous methods in an initial API more often than not creates confusion for newcomers on how the API works and is supposed to be used. This is contrary to the argument that having this method makes life easier for developers. A convenience method it may be, but it still enlarges the schema to the API.

Such convenience methods do typically belong in porcelain APIs. I don't see OpenTracing having really dealt with the plumbing vs porcelain, nor SPI vs API, contraints of API design properly yet. That leaves me a little uneasy about adding such superfluous methods that limit our horizon.

So, it's not that I can't see its value as a convenient method within a porcelain api, it's just that the plumbing api needs to be finialised first, before we start hooking up all the porcelain to it.

For example, as we work with solely a plumbing api, we may find (just like in the merger of start_trace and join_trace) that it can be simplified and `start_child` becomes completely redundant.

The library uses the best available connection.  It will connect insecurely only when running on a platform where a secure connection can not be made.  When it does so it will log a warning message indicating that a secure connection was not possible.

There is no built-in option to override this logic.  Insecure connection is only available as a fallback for when a secure connection is not possible.  Advanced users _could_ force an insecure connection to be used by making changes to a few lines of code/removing the required SSL libraries if they really want to.

#1007 should solve this: it essentially makes `py::module_local` only apply to the C++ -> Python conversion, while the Python -> C++ conversion will try to load from all local, foreign-local, and global type casters associated with the C++ type.

So, my main points here are:

* The `cmake install` method should not be affected -- it should not install headers into Python version-specific directories. That is addressed by the latest commit by making it optional and not default. Great!

* `conda install` can also invoke `cmake` and therefore install the config files into `PREFIX/share/cmake/pybind11`. The only concern raised by others is if the headers should be installed into `PREFIX/include/pythonX.Y/pybind11` or `PREFIX/include/pybind11`. I'd say that this does not matter for `conda install` because the `PREFIX` will always be managed by a conda environment which is going to enforce a strict single Python version per environment, so there will be no way for conflicts to happen. I previously objected to this because this affected the pure `cmake install` method which could have conflicting Python versions. But the latest commit resolves that by making it `conda`-only.

* There are no changes to the `pip install` method. I only have one question here: Is there any practical way to also get the config files installed with `pip`? But this is probably not important now that it's clear that this PR primarily targets the `conda install` method.
Eigen's is configurable at compile time, but yes, the default is signed.

> @SylvainCorlay  EDIT: would you want an option to do so? (I don't think that it would be a good idea for the reasons above).

> @jagerman achieve the desired symmetry by always installing to ${PREFIX}/include (which seems pretty reasonable given that there is absolutely nothing python-version-dependent in the header files).

My point too, headers are completely interpreter-version-independent. Also, with conda in mind, all conda packages I've ever seen (bar numpy) install non-Python-specific headers to `$PREFIX/include` which I think was a great decision, because `-isystem $PREFIX/include` and you're done with it - this works with any interpreter version, and is completely CMake-independent. For this reason we currently have to package pybind11 manually so it gets installed to environment's `include` folder.

Other true miscellany determined in the course of my research:
* Saying `--index-url` (even without disabling update checking) prevents hitting pypi.python.org.
* Having an unreachable PyPI address hard-coded in pip (as pypi.python.org is) or specified as `--index-url` will totally scuttle any success; pip will not go on to try anything specified with `--extra-index-url`. So we can't just stack up a bunch of mirrors and expect pip to go down the list.

Thanks for looking into this @joohoi.

Digging into it a bit myself, I think in almost all scenarios we don't need to or shouldn't do anything here. According to Apache's [documentation](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.4/mod/mod_ssl.html#sslcertificatefile) and [changelog](https://svn.apache.org/repos/asf/httpd/httpd/branches/2.4.x/CHANGES), starting with Apache 2.4.7, it automatically chooses DH parameters based on your RSA key size.

I confirmed this behavior testing on Ubuntu 16.04. Using a 2048-bit RSA key (which is Certbot's default), 2048-bit DH params are used. Using a 4096-bit key, 4096-bit DH params are used. Based on [Alex Halderman's comment about using widely shared 2048-bit params](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/374#issuecomment-114236157) and the fact that using our own 2048-bit parameters would result in a weaker key being used if the user selected a larger key size, I think Apache 2.4.7's default behavior might be good enough/preferred.

As for the distros out there using Apache 2.2.x, [Red Hat backported this change to RHEL/CentOS 6](https://wiki.mozilla.org/Security/TLS_Configurations#Apache) which I confirmed works. I unfortunately couldn't (quickly) find any documentation about a backport to Debian 7 Wheezy (old old stable) or RHEL/CentOS 7, but I observed larger keys being used on both Debian and CentOS 7 despite the Apache version.

For older Apache systems that do not have this behavior, I'm not sure what we can do. My understanding is the ability to specify custom DH params came with the behavior of automatically using larger params based on your key size in Apache 2.4.7. I think the best we could do is to try and get the user on a new version of Apache.

One other scenario where this approach might not work is for LibreSSL users based on [this page](https://weakdh.org/sysadmin.html). We could potentially do something for this case in the future if Apache does not, but I personally don't think it's worth changing our entire approach here.

@fuzzmz,

- [gpg.sh](https://github.com/escapewindow/docker-signing-server/blob/master/gpg.sh) - This is a helper script to run gpg with a given gpg homedir.  The gpg homedir defaults to `~/.gnupg`, but we create a bunch of gpg homedirs in scriptworker.  It's helpful to be able to run things like `--list-keys` in each gpg homedir without having to remember the exact commandline arguments to do so.
    - We need to be able to specify a gpg binary, e.g. `/usr/local/bin/gpg2`. This will be specific to each machine/environment, so this should be in an env var or commandline option.
    - We need to be able to specify a gpg homedir, e.g. `~/.gnupg` or `./tmp-gnupg-home`.  In the [example `gpg.sh`](https://github.com/escapewindow/docker-signing-server/blob/master/gpg.sh), that's hardcoded to `docker/gpg`.  We need to be able to support any gpg homedir, so this should be specifiable by env var or commandline option.

- [gpg.py](https://bug1298553.bmoattachments.org/attachment.cgi?id=8792961) - This is a helper script to create and export gpg keypairs.
    - We probably want commandline options for `GPG_HOME`, `GPG_PATH`, and `HOSTS`.  The script currently hardcodes `cltsign@{host}` as the email address; we should allow for a different username in the commandline options.
    - options on whether to `shutil.rmtree` or not, and to change the default expiration from `2y` would be helpful as well.

The current names aren't very descriptive; we could rename the latter to `create_gpg_keys.py` or something?  I'm also not sure where to put them -- a `contrib/` or `helper_scripts` dir?  We'd want to make sure to package them, which means we'd add them in `MANIFEST.in`.

My memory is a bit hazy, and I don't have time to research the details, but from what I recall your problem is here: `sqlalchemy_session_persistence = 'commit'`

As soon as the Author is created, it's immediately committed. Instead, I would set that to `None` or `'flush'`, depending on what you're trying to achieve. A flush will hit the DB, but inside of the transaction, so you can roll it back at the end of your test / dev work if you want. `None` might also help with your desired behavior, it really depends on whether you're trying to altogether avoid a DB hit or just keep it nested within a transaction.

I also had similar issues, this is what i learned.

I can confirm that when the UPN is part of the jwt, the errors goes away as the UID now is defined. To see whether the UID is set, enable 'social_core.pipeline.debug.debug' in your pipeline which should print the UID to stdout. If it's not set (None value), it's most likely due to the configuration of the Azure account.

If you set up your Azure account from scratch for testing, you can try these steps from within the portal.azure.com to enable UPN data for a user (NB! This is a scenario that worked for me to test my multi-tenant web-app. Your requirements/setup may be totally different!):

1) Create the App: "Active Directory" -> "App registrations" -> "New application"
2) Setup a unique domain: "Active directory" -> "Add custom domain" and verify it.
3) Create a new group: "Active Directory" -> "New group"
4) Bind app to group via self-service: "Active Directory" -> "Enterprise applications" -> Select your app -> "Self-service" -> YES to "Allow users to request .." + select the group you made in "To which group ..."
5) Create a new test-user within your AD using the domain name in 2)

When that test-user signs in, it will automatically be added to the group you specified and the oauth-2 jwt will include the UPN.

Since some azure accounts may give these errors I added a requirement in my pipeline so that it would raise error if upn field is not found.

I believe you're asking about the following situation, where we launch some tasks, and then call `ray.get` on the results one after another.

```python
import ray
import time

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def f():
  time.sleep(1)
  return 1

# Launch some tasks.
results = []
for i in range(4):
  results.append(f.remote())

# Get all of the results.
for i in range(4):
  ray.get(results[i])
```

Is that correct?

All of the calls to `f` can be executed in parallel. The reason for this is that **execution in Ray is not lazy**, and so it will not wait for a call to `ray.get` to begin executing a task.

You're right that `ray.get` will block until the object is available. However, `ray.get` does not actually *trigger* the execution of the task.

To demonstrate this, you can try waiting a little bit before running the second for loop and it should return immediately.

```
class LSTM(Model):
    """Adds a LSTM cell on top of some other model output.

    Uses a linear layer at the end for output.

    Important: we assume inputs is a padded batch of sequences denoted by
        self.seq_lens. See add_time_dimension() for more information.
    """

    def _build_layers(self, inputs, num_outputs, options):
        cell_size = options.get("lstm_cell_size", 256)
        use_tf100_api = (distutils.version.LooseVersion(tf.VERSION) >=
                         distutils.version.LooseVersion("1.0.0"))
        last_layer = add_time_dimension(inputs, self.seq_lens)

        # Setup the LSTM cell
        if use_tf100_api:
            lstm1 = rnn.BasicLSTMCell(cell_size, state_is_tuple=True)
            lstm2 = rnn.BasicLSTMCell(cell_size, state_is_tuple=True)
        else:
            lstm1 = rnn.rnn_cell.BasicLSTMCell(cell_size, state_is_tuple=True)
            lstm2 = rnn.rnn_cell.BasicLSTMCell(cell_size, state_is_tuple=True)
        self.state_init = [
            np.zeros(lstm1.state_size.c, np.float32),
            np.zeros(lstm1.state_size.h, np.float32),
            np.zeros(lstm2.state_size.c, np.float32),
            np.zeros(lstm2.state_size.h, np.float32)
        ]

        # Setup LSTM inputs
        if self.state_in:
            c1_in, h1_in, c2_in, h2_in = self.state_in
        else:
            c1_in = tf.placeholder(
                tf.float32, [None, lstm1.state_size.c], name="c1")
            h1_in = tf.placeholder(
                tf.float32, [None, lstm1.state_size.h], name="h1")
            c2_in = tf.placeholder(
                tf.float32, [None, lstm2.state_size.c], name="c2")
            h2_in = tf.placeholder(
                tf.float32, [None, lstm2.state_size.h], name="h2")
            self.state_in = [c1_in, h1_in, c2_in, h2_in]

        # Setup LSTM outputs
        if use_tf100_api:
            state1_in = rnn.LSTMStateTuple(c1_in, h1_in)
            state2_in = rnn.LSTMStateTuple(c2_in, h2_in)
        else:
            state1_in = rnn.rnn_cell.LSTMStateTuple(c1_in, h1_in)
            state2_in = rnn.rnn_cell.LSTMStateTuple(c2_in, h2_in)
        lstm1_out, lstm1_state = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(
            lstm1,
            last_layer,
            sequence_length=self.seq_lens,
            time_major=False,
            dtype=tf.float32)

        with tf.variable_scope("value_function"):
            lstm2_out, lstm2_state = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(
                lstm2,
                last_layer,
                sequence_length=self.seq_lens,
                time_major=False,
                dtype=tf.float32)

        self.value_function = tf.reshape(
            linear(
                tf.reshape(lstm2_out, [-1, cell_size]),
                1, "vf", normc_initializer(0.01)),
            [-1])

        self.state_out = list(lstm1_state) + list(lstm2_state)

        # Compute outputs
        last_layer = tf.reshape(lstm1_out, [-1, cell_size])
        logits = linear(last_layer, num_outputs, "action",
                        normc_initializer(0.01))

        return logits, last_layer
```

The only thing which could be done to improve the situation of rapidly changing plugins is having a different release model. According to [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/4K_resolution), actually 4K = 2160p; the term "4K" refers to horizontal resolution (= 3840, i.e. UHD-1). "4320p" is [8K](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/8K_resolution), horizontal resolution being 7680, a format Youtube, AFAIK, does not provide...

```
140          m4a        audio only DASH audio  128k , m4a_dash container, mp4a.40.2@128k (44100Hz), 4.67MiB

171          webm       audio only DASH audio  171k , vorbis@128k (44100Hz), 5.01MiB

266          mp4        3840x2160  DASH video 23868k , avc1.640033, 30fps, video only, 669.87MiB
138          mp4        3840x2160  DASH video 24322k , avc1.640033, 30fps, video only, 573.82MiB
```

I'm coming around to your point of view that storing the information in the constants dictionary may not be the best approach.

Come to think of it, I think this discussion was not necessary -- a more reliable source of the model top pressure is the hybrid coordinate pair a and b -- the model top pressure is given by Eq. 7 in the Eckermann paper when eta = 0, which is simply `a[-1] + b[-1]*reference_pressure`. For numerical reasons you want `b[-1] = 0`, so `a[-1]` gives us the model top pressure. Somebody really needs to search for and modify this variable, so we can assume they know what they are doing.

This gives the advantage that components don't need to store model top pressure at any point in time, and `state` has all the information to verify its own consistency.

So, shall we do this? use the coordinates to infer model top pressure for calculations with GFS and Dcmip (for now)?

>  In my ideal world, networking could act similarly with, sendall blocking until recv (or multiple calls to it) had read the entire single message on the far end. Is there any way to do this without waiting for an application level per-message ack?

No, if these are the semantics you want then a per-message ack is exactly the way to implement it. TCP doesn't provide this information otherwise; the only information the sender gets is a notification of when the data has entered the remote side's kernel receive buffer, not when it's been read out.

Note also that the policy you has a really extreme bias towards latency over throughput. That may well make sense for your application, but TCP doesn't know that :-). Like, imagine if file downloads worked like this: you'd send 1 packet, then you'd wait for an acknowledgement, then you'd send the next packet, etc. It's entirely realistic for big international fiber connections to be able to push 1 gigabyte per second, with 100 ms+ transmission latency. With your model, on a pipe like that, you get to send 1 packet every 200 ms. That's like less than 10 kilobytes per second. If you want to do better than that, then you *have* to be willing to speculatively push more data into the pipe and hope that everything works out. But then if you push too much, your latency dies.

The challenge for folks working on stuff like TCP and routers and etc. is to design algorithms so that they can get good throughput *and* good latency at the same time. And to do it in this horribly messy system where every router in between you and the other side has its own buffers, and then there's the TCP control loop on top, and etc. etc. And there's actually been massive massive progress on this in the last few years, but it requires changes all through the system. Curio/Trio's approach is one part of that, and a critical part, but unfortunately we can't magically fix all the networking equipment in the world :-(.

Yeah, that's an awkward bit for sure... the history is that before v0.3.0, it *didn't* reduce, because you weren't allowed to do arbitrary amounts of work inside a nursery block, and this really was a case where passing nurseries around was necessary. But then we fixed that, so the example stopped making much sense, and I stuck in an awkward patch instead of rethinking the section to find a new example...

I'm actually having trouble thinking of a simple case where passing nurseries around is actually useful... I can think of some exotic ones, like "implementing trio-asyncio", but that's not a great way to introduce the concept :-).

Actually, I misunderstood: `getnameinfo(("127.0.0.1", 80), NI_NUMERICHOST)` gives `("127.0.0.1", "http")`, i.e. it's exactly `getservbyport`. So yeah, let's kill 'em.

Another point: `getfqdn` should probably go too: it uses the obsolete `gethostbyaddr`, and AFAICT you can do the same thing better with `getaddrinfo(..., AI_CANONNAME)`. (And otherwise we need to copy the implementation into our code and switch it to use `getaddrinfo`, because IDNA.)

And `getnameinfo` does an implicit `getaddrinfo` if you pass in a string name. So we should probably do the force-numeric-address check on it, like we do for other functions taking an address.

An additional benefit here is that if people switch around from ``serve_tcp``, to ``serve_ssl_over_tcp``, to ``serve_listeners``, I imagine it's not uncommon to accidentally send the wrong parameters into the wrong function. Presumably when that happens now, you either get a too many/too few parameter exception or I'm assuming duck typing takes over and you get some weird error when you try to use an integer as a listener or something like that.

However, with mandatory kwargs, you get much better error messages, something like: ``TypeError: serve_listeners() got an unexpected keyword argument 'port'`` instead. That seems like an additional win to me.
